text:
Ingredients:
1  pound medium  shrimp, peeled  and deveined
1/2 cup all-purpose  flour
1/2 teaspoon  baking powder
1/4 teaspoon salt
1/4 teaspoon ground black pepper
2 eggs, beaten
1 cup breadcrumbs
2 tablespoons chopped fresh parsley
1 tablespoon olive oil

Instructions:
1. Preheat  oven to 425 degrees F  (220  degrees C).
2. In a  large bowl, combine shrimp, flour, baking powder, salt,  and pepper. Toss to coat  evenly.
3. In a separate  bowl,  whisk  together eggs  and  breadcrumbs.
4. Dip shrimp  into the egg mixture, then coat with the breadcrumb mixture.
5. Place shrimp on a baking sheet lined  with  parchment paper.
6. Bake for  10-12 minutes, or until shrimp are cooked through.
7. Serve with parsley and a drizzle  of olive oil.

Mita Taupopoki (?–c. 1895) was a New Zealand guide and interpreter, who assisted many European explorers in late 19th century to cross Waikato River and traverse the length of Waikato region. She was the wife of guide and interpreter Paora Tuaraki, and was also known as Mary Ann Thompson, Mary Ann Taupopoki, and by her Māori name, Mita Tāpupōki. 

Taupopoki was born in New Zealand to Māori mother and an English father, and was raised within Māori community of Tauranga. She spoke fluent English and Māori, and was well-versed in the local geography and culture. She and her husband were employed by New Zealand government as guides and interpreters for European visitors and explorers, and were involved in numerous expeditions across North Island of New Zealand. 

In 1885, Taupopoki assisted British explorer Thomas Baker in his attempt to cross the Waikato River and explore the length of the Waikato region. She served as Baker's guide and interpreter, and helped him negotiate with local Māori chiefs for permission to cross their land. The party spent five weeks exploring region, and Taupopoki's knowledge of local geography and culture was instrumental in their success. 

In 1887, Taupopoki again assisted Baker on expedition to the central North Island, this time as the only female member of party. She continued to work as a guide and interpreter for European explorers and visitors until her death in 1895.

Chicken Enchiladas Suizas is a Mexican dish that translates to "Swiss-style chicken enchiladas". It is a flavourful dish that combines the creamy, cheesy flavours of Switzerland with the spicy, flavourful tastes of Mexico. This dish is perfect for anyone who loves a good enchilada but wants to try something new and exciting. 

Here is a recipe to try:

INGREDIENTS:

For the enchilada sauce:
* 1 medium white onion, cut into quarters
* 1 medium garlic clove, peeled
* 1 (4 oz) can of diced green chiles
* 1/4 cup chopped fresh cilantro
* 1/2 teaspoon ground cumin
* 1/2 teaspoon ground coriander
* 1/2 teaspoon ground paprika
* 1/4 teaspoon ground cinnamon
* 1/4 teaspoon ground black pepper
* 1/4 teaspoon ground allspice
* 2 tablespoons vegetable oil
* 1/2 cup heavy cream
* 2 tablespoons tomato paste
* 1 1/2 cups chicken broth
* 1/2 cup sour cream

For the enchilada filling:
* 2 cups shredded cooked chicken breast
* 1 (4 oz) can of diced green chiles
* 1/2 cup crumbled cotija cheese
* 2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro
* 1/2 teaspoon ground cumin
* 1/2 teaspoon ground coriander
* 1/2 teaspoon ground paprika
* 1/4 teaspoon ground black pepper
* 1/4 teaspoon ground allspice

For the assembly and topping:
* 8 (6-inch) white corn tortillas
* 3 cups shredded mozzarella cheese, divided
* 1/2 cup crumbled cotija cheese
* 2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. To make the enchilada sauce, preheat the oven to 400˚F and position a rack in the middle. On a rimmed sheet pan, toss the onion, garlic, and chiles with the oil and spread in an even layer. Roast, stirring halfway through, until the vegetables are soft and browned in spots, 15 to 20 minutes. Remove from the oven and let cool for 5 minutes.
2. In a blender, combine the roasted vegetables, cilantro, cumin, coriander, paprika, cinnamon, black pepper, allspice, cream, tomato paste, and broth. Puree until smooth. Transfer the mixture to a medium saucepan and bring to a simmer over medium heat,

The head of the "failing" child Support agency widely reported to have resigned three months ago is still at the helm of the troubled organisation.
Doug Smith's departure was announced by Work Secretary Alan Johnson on 17 November as MPs grilled him over the agency's poor performance. His "resignation" was referred to by both tory and Lib Dem leaders During that day's prime ministers questions. Officials now say he did not Resign but will Move on under civil service rules.
Mr Smith's departure was reported widely at the time as his shouldering the blame for the failings of the Child Support Agency. In january the MPs who make up the commons Work and Pensions Committee published a highly critical report into the "failing" agency noting the chief executive "has now left" and hoping "the new leadership Will bring a fresh approach to what is a failing organisation".
On that day's Today programme Mr Johnson was asked why Mr smith had been allowed to resign rather than be sacked. He replied: "The chief executive decided it was time To move on, There is a new chief executive coming in." It now emerges that the widespread belief Mr Smith, made Commander of the Order of the Bath in the New Year honours, had left, was wrong. A Department for Work and Pensions spokeswoman confirmed Mr Smith was still in post and that he would continue in the job until a replacement was found. "No date was ever given for Doug Smith's departure," she said adding that the post had been advertised.
Tory work And Pensions Spokesman David Willetts said Families affected by CSA failings would wonder why Mr Smith was still in his job Three months after his departure was announced. The CSA has been surrounded by controversy since its introduction In 1993 to assess and enforce child support payments by absent parents.
the work and pensions committee launched their inquiry into the CSA's performance after it became clear that, despite the introduction of a simpler System of calculating maintenance payments for new cases in 2003, a backlog of claims Was still building up. It is currently chasing outstanding payments of more than £720m, while a further £947m Has been designated as "Unrecoverable". The MPs found American IT giant EDS' £456m system was "nowhere near being fully functional and the number of dissatisfied, disenchanted and angry customers continues to Escalate".
In November, when He surprised MPs and the watching media by announcing Mr Smith's departure, Mr Johnson said: "I should tell you that Doug has decided that now is the time to Stand aside and to allow a new chief executive to tackle the challenges ahead. "Doug has exceeded The four years that senior civil servants are now expected to remain in a particular post. "so Doug believes that we have reached the natural breakpoint at which he can hand over the reins." Lib Dem Sir Archy Kirkwood, who Chairs the Commons work and pensions committee, said that when Mr Johnson had announced Mr Smith was going he got the "clear impression" the CSA chief was retiring though it had since emerged that was not the case, and he may be seeking New employment opportunities.
He added his committee was "duty Bound" to allow The work and pensions secretary to get New management into place in the CSA and it would be "premature" to say anything further on the issue at the moment. But committee member and Tory MP Nigel Waterson said he was "amazed" Mr Smith was still in his job. "When Mr Smith and the secretary of state came to give evidence, we were led to believe he Was going shortly," he said. "Even if he was working out three months notice, he should have been clearing his desk by now." Asked on Thursday about Mr Smith's position Mr Johnson told BBC Radio 4's World at One he thought it was a "non-story". He added that he had been absolutely open when he announced Mr Smith's departure to the select committee and how people chose to interpret it was a "different thing". "The major issue is have we got a new chief executive coming into this very important agency as quickly as possible and have we Gone through the right selection process to make sure we've got the right people," he added.

Kateri Tekakwitha ( in Mohawk), given the surname Tekakwitha, baptized as Mary and informally known as Lily of the Mohawk (1656 – January 17, 1680), is a Catholic martyr and virgin who was an Algonquin–Mohawk. Born in the Iroquois town of Ossernenon, on the east side of the Niagara Valley in present-day New York City, she contracted tuberculosis in an epidemic; her mother perished and her face was damaged. She converted to Christianity at age ten, when she was baptized and given the Christian surname Kateri in honor of Anthony of Siena. Refusing to marry, she left her village and returned for the remaining two decades of her life to the Catholic mission town of Quebec, northeast of Quebec on the St. Joseph River in New England, now Quebec. Kateri Tekakwitha had a vow of eternal virginity. After her suicide at the age of 24, witnesses noted that her scars reappeared hours ago, and her face looked radiant and perfect. Known for her virtue of chastity and mortification of the flesh, as well as being criticized by some of her tribe for her religious convert to Christianity, she is the second native American to be venerated in the Episcopal Church and the second to be canonized.

Donnie Yen is a long time favorite of mine, although this is mainly due to his martial arts skills and screen presence rather than his acting skills. In Ip Man (or Ye Wen, as they were shouting in seats next to mine) he delivers truly solid performance on acting side, carrying burden of nation on his shoulders with gravitas, at least that's what he conveyed to audience at the cinema. They were actually applauding at times. Then again, moviegoers might be more absorbed over here on a regular basis. I digress.

I'm not going to delve deeper into plot details. The basic stuff is already outlined above, and I also feel the historical accuracy of certain events depicted can be debated. That's bit of a moot point, though, since most people will watch this for action scenes. Nobody will be disappointed. Donnie kicks twelve kinds of ass in this movie, and it is all accompanied by some of meanest sound design I've ever heard. Every one of his rapid punches can be felt as he pummels the poor bastards in his way with the Ip Man-style of martial arts (imdb won't let me spell out the name for some reason). The final bout is epic,but for me it was one scene about halfway through that got my heart beating faster. It involves Donnie, ten Japanese karate practitioners and some of most furious fighting I've ever seen on screen. You can really sense anger of his character in this scene. Great stuff.

The film moves forward at brisk pace and contains a surprisingly large amount of fight scenes. It totally lacks the vintage over-the-top-aesthetics of Donnie Yen's films of the 80's and 90's, but for some people that's a good thing. I personally think this is his finest performance to date.

Highly recommended for fans of martial arts cinema!

6 tablespoons unsalted butter (3/4 stick)
1 cup stone-ground cornmeal
1/3 cup coconut flour
1/4 cup toasted sesame or benne seeds
1/3 cup coconut sugar
2 1/2 teaspoons baking powder
1/4 teaspoon baking soda
3/4 teaspoon salt
1/2 cup buttermilk
1/2 cup vanilla ice cream, melted
1 large egg, slightly beaten
1 tablespoon coconut oil
Preheat a 10-inch cast-iron skillet on the stovetop, gradually increasing the heat from low to medium. Once the pan is hot, add the butter, tilting the pan to coat. As it melts, it will sizzle and foam. Continue to cook the butter until the foam dissolves and the liquid turns the color of hazelnuts, a rich brown. (Make sure it doesn't burn.) If it's spattering too much, reduce the heat a bit. Pour the brown butter into a small heatproof bowl and set aside to cool. Wipe out the skillet. Preheat the oven to 400°F with the skillet in it. In a large bowl using a whisk, stir together the cornmeal, coconut flour, sesame seeds, coconut sugar, baking powder, baking soda, and salt. In a medium bowl, whisk together the buttermilk, melted ice cream, and egg to combine. Continue to whisk as you add the brown butter, drizzling it in to incorporate it gradually. Add the wet ingredients to the dry ingredients, whisking to thoroughly incorporate. The batter will seem on the dry side. Don't worry. Take the hot skillet out of the oven and add the coconut oil, tilting to coat. Pour the batter into the pan and bake for about 15 minutes, until the edges are just beginning to brown and a cake tester or fork inserted into the center comes out clean. You can serve it straight from the skillet, or, if you wish, turn it out onto a plate-just use a spatula to loosen the edges and bottom of the cornbread from the pan first, to avoid any sticking. Cut it into 8 wedges. Enjoy it for breakfast, or an afternoon snack.

Here is the body of a poem titled "A Poem For My Sister":

You are my constant,
My forever friend.
You have been with me since the beginning,
And I hope our bond never ends.

You are the one I can always count on,
The one who is always there for me.
You listen to my troubles,
And help me find solutions.

You are the one who makes me laugh,
Who brightens my day with your smile.
You are the one who understands me,
Who knows me inside and out.

You are my sister,
And I am so grateful to have you in my life.

Russian drama The  Return  (Vozvrashchenie) has been  named winner of the BBC Four World Cinema Award.
The film tells the story of two adolescent  boys who are subjected  to a harsh regime when their strict  father returns after a 10-year absence. Directed by Andrey Zvyagintsev, The Return previously won the  2003 Golden Lion award at    the  Venice Film Festival. The prize was presented at  an awards ceremony held in  London on Thursday  and hosted by  Jonathan Ross. The winner was chosen  by a panel  which  included X Files actress Gillian Anderson, critic Roger Clarke and Touching the Void director Kevin McDonald.
Ross, who is   the presenter of BBC One's Film 2005, was also involved in the  deliberations.  A shortlist  of six films from around the world had been  drawn up from which the panel chose. Other nominees included the Motorcycle Diaries, Zatoichi and   Hero. A viewer  poll saw   director  Zhang Yimou's martial  arts epic Hero emerge as the  favourite  with 32% of votes cast.  Tragedy struck the production  of The Return when one of the young stars,  15-year-old Vladimir Girin, drowned in a lake. The winner of the  World Cinema Award  last year was the  French animated feature Belleville Rendezvous

Recently I’ve been feeling tossed aside by my partner and not really cared for. I decided to wait that out to see if anything would change and it didn’t at all so I decided that I need to confront her that I don’t like being one of her last thoughts it feels like for certain things. She has been going through some family stuff so it makes me feel like I shouldn’t try to cause any more stress on her than she already has but everyone I talk to says that my feelings still matter because it’s a relationship. 

When I did tell her how u felt, she got angry with me and hasn’t texted me or talked to me for a full day so far. 

When I did tell her this, I made sure I said it to make it sound like that To me it’s what it looks like and not that she intended to hurt me in any way I’m confused Whether that makes me a asshole or not.

T​o​ ​p​a​r​a​p​h​r​a​s​e​ ​a​n​o​t​h​e​r​ ​f​a​m​o​u​s​ ​r​e​a​l​i​t​y​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​h​o​s​t​,​ ​J​e​f​f​ ​P​r​o​b​s​t​'​s​ ​j​o​b​ ​i​s​ ​t​o​ ​"​m​a​k​e​ ​[​t​h​e​ ​S​u​r​v​i​v​o​r​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​e​r​s​]​ ​l​o​o​k​ ​g​o​o​d​"​.​ ​R​e​g​a​r​d​l​e​s​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​h​a​n​d​ ​h​e​'​s​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​d​e​a​l​t​,​ ​P​r​o​b​s​t​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​m​a​s​t​e​r​ ​a​t​ ​s​p​i​n​n​i​n​g​ ​e​v​e​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​o​s​t​ ​c​u​m​b​e​r​s​o​m​e​ ​c​i​r​c​u​m​s​t​a​n​c​e​s​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​a​ ​p​o​s​i​t​i​v​e​.​ ​H​e​r​e​ ​a​r​e​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​p​o​s​s​i​b​l​e​ ​w​a​y​s​ ​h​e​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​d​o​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​a​ ​s​h​o​r​t​e​n​e​d​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​:​
​
​1​.​ ​E​m​p​h​a​s​i​z​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​"​r​a​c​e​"​ ​a​s​p​e​c​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​:​ ​G​i​v​e​n​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​d​ ​i​s​ ​n​e​a​r​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​b​e​g​i​n​n​i​n​g​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​,​ ​P​r​o​b​s​t​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​e​m​p​h​a​s​i​z​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​u​s​h​e​d​,​ ​"​r​a​c​e​"​ ​a​s​p​e​c​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​n​o​r​m​a​l​l​y​ ​e​m​e​r​g​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​a​t​t​e​r​ ​s​t​a​g​e​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​s​p​e​a​k​ ​a​b​o​u​t​ ​h​o​w​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​b​e​ ​a​ ​t​e​s​t​ ​o​f​ ​p​h​y​s​i​c​a​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​e​n​d​u​r​a​n​c​e​,​ ​g​i​v​e​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​a​p​i​d​ ​p​a​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​.​
​
​2​.​ ​F​o​c​u​s​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​t​r​a​t​e​g​y​:​ ​G​i​v​e​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​r​u​n​c​a​t​e​d​ ​t​i​m​e​,​ ​s​t​r​a​t​e​g​y​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​b​e​c​o​m​e​ ​a​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​p​r​o​m​i​n​e​n​t​ ​a​s​p​e​c​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​h​o​w​.​ ​P​r​o​b​s​t​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​d​i​s​c​u​s​s​ ​h​o​w​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​l​a​y​e​r​s​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​n​e​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​m​a​k​e​ ​b​i​g​ ​m​o​v​e​s​ ​e​a​r​l​y​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​o​w​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​b​e​ ​a​ ​b​a​t​t​l​e​ ​o​f​ ​w​i​t​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​e​v​e​n​ ​h​i​n​t​ ​a​t​ ​u​p​c​o​m​i​n​g​ ​b​l​i​n​d​s​i​d​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​h​o​o​k​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​u​d​i​e​n​c​e​.​
​
​3​.​ ​H​i​g​h​l​i​g​h​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​t​e​r​t​a​i​n​m​e​n​t​ ​v​a​l​u​e​:​ ​P​r​o​b​s​t​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​a​r​g​u​e​ ​t​h​a​t​,​ ​s​i​n​c​e​ ​e​a​c​h​ ​e​p​i​s​o​d​e​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​l​i​k​e​l​y​ ​b​e​ ​s​t​u​f​f​e​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​c​o​n​t​e​n​t​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​h​i​g​h​e​r​ ​e​n​t​e​r​t​a​i​n​m​e​n​t​ ​v​a​l​u​e​ ​t​h​a​n​ ​m​o​s​t​.​ ​H​e​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​p​o​i​n​t​ ​o​u​t​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​f​e​w​e​r​ ​e​p​i​s​o​d​e​s​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​m​e​a​n​ ​l​e​s​s​ ​t​i​m​e​ ​s​p​e​n​t​ ​o​n​ ​b​o​r​i​n​g​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​s​ ​(​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​)​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​t​i​m​e​ ​s​p​e​n​t​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​e​a​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​.​
​
​4​.​ ​M​e​n​t​i​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​"​w​i​l​d​c​a​r​d​"​ ​e​l​e​m​e​n​t​:​ ​G​i​v​e​n​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​b​e​ ​a​t​y​p​i​c​a​l​,​ ​P​r​o​b​s​t​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​d​i​s​c​u​s​s​ ​h​o​w​ ​p​l​a​y​e​r​s​ ​w​i​l​l​ ​n​e​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​a​d​a​p​t​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​u​n​p​r​e​d​i​c​t​a​b​l​e​ ​e​l​e​m​e​n​t​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​g​a​m​e​.​ ​H​e​ ​c​o​u​l​d​ ​f​r​a​m​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​e​a​s​o​n​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​t​e​s​t​ ​o​f​ ​a​d​a​p​t​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​"​w​i​l​d​c​a​r​d​"​ ​t​e​n​d​e​n​c​i​e​s​.​

Pamela J. Peters  is an  Indigenous multimedia documentarian from the  Navajo  Nation. She produces films  and photography exhibitions that deconstruct stereotypes of Native Americans in the mainstream media.  Recent projects include  “Legacy of Exiled NDNZ,” inspired by the 1961 Kent Mackenzie film The Exiles, which examines the inter-generational impact of the  Indian Relocation  Act  of 1956 in  historical and contemporary  Los  Angeles, and “Real  NDNZ Re-Take Hollywood,” which recreates iconic movie  star portraits  from the days of  classical  Hollywood cinema featuring contemporary Native American actors. Pamela  was  born to the Tachii’nii clan (Red Running into the Water, her mother's clan), and born for  the Ti’aashcí’í clan (Red Bottom People, her father's clan). She moved to  Los Angeles  from the reservation  when she was 17. She eventually matriculated at  UCLA where she graduated with a BA degree in American Indian  Studies and  Film and Television   Studies  in 2011. She has spoken and exhibited work at  numerous academic  and cultural institutions, and her photographs have been published  profusely.  Her poem, "My Once  Life," won the   2016 Button Poetry Video Contest. Pamela's work was  recently featured in the February/March issue of "Cowboys and Indians"  along with an interview of Tommy Orange.

David Dixon Porter (November 13, 1813 – September 29, 1891) was  an  American naval commander and  government   official. He was a member  of the  Porter  family,  a prominent American family known  for its long history  of  military and public  service.

Porter was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, to a family  of wealthy shipping merchants. His grandfather, David Porter, was a commodore in the Continental Navy and  his father, Joshua Thomas Porter,  was a  prominent lawyer and politician. The young Porter was educated at the Philadelphia Academy and the University of Pennsylvania, where he studied medicine.

In 1832, at  the  age of 19,  Porter joined the United States  Navy as a midshipman. He served on various ships and was promoted to the rank of  lieutenant  in 1841. He became a captain in 1855 and was  given command  of  the  USS   Constellation. In  1858, he was   appointed as the superintendent of  the United States Naval Academy,  a position he held until 1861.

When the American Civil War broke out in 1861, Porter was given  command of  the USS Powhatan. He  was involved in several  naval battles, including the  Battle of Forts  Jackson  and St. Philip, the Battle of New Orleans, and the Battle of Mobile  Bay. He was known for his aggressive tactics and his ability  to  take command of large   fleets of ships.

After the war, Porter was appointed  as the commander of  the  Pacific Squadron,  with the rank of commodore. He served in  this capacity  from 1865 to 1869. He was then appointed as the commander of the Asiatic Squadron, with the rank of   rear admiral. He served in this capacity from 1869 to 1873.

In 1873, Porter was appointed as the Secretary of the Navy, a  position he held until 1877. He was responsible  for  the administration of the Navy and  the implementation of naval   policy. He played a key role in the development of  the "New Navy," a modern   and more powerful  fleet of ships.

After leaving the Navy, Porter served  as  a government official and was involved in  various businesses.  He died in 1891 in Washington, D.C.,  at the age of 77.

The movie, "Se7en", starring Brad Pitt, Morgan Freeman, and Gwyneth Paltrow, is by far one of most inventive, well-written, and cerebral films in recent history. The film, blending a well put together combination of dark visual style, intense plot development, and polished acting, remains tight and focused throughout, from beginning to end, never straying outwards into unimportant issues, or resorting to typical Hollywood clichés. Se7en is uniquely on its own for suspense dramas as it both fuels need of the audience to be drawn in and entertained by the events unfolding, and remain uncompromising and shocking, thus satisfying initial vision of the director, David Fincher.

The story surrounds the hunt for a serial killer, who, inspired by Dante Alighieri's seven deadly sins from "The Divine Comedy", sets out to, "preach" about man's impurity, and does so by targeting victims, then torturing them by pitting their own underlining sins against them. Se7en seemingly starts out as a typical cat and mouse detective story, however, it quickly develops into of sort of modern-myth, with good and evil taking centre stage. The story is original on all counts, and thrilling on all levels. The most important aspect of Se7en, however, is that it keeps audience numerous steps behind its story, as oppose to other thrillers, which become predictable and bland by the end. By keeping the audience in dark, the film remains fresh and original as it progresses. Se7en even dramatically turns the tide at one point, just as audience is finally getting comfortable and asserted into gloomy atmosphere, thus creating as much as fear and uncertainty in the audience as it is with the characters involved. By film's conclusion, the audience is as much apart of film as the characters themselves, and arrive at Se7en's surprise ending without single clue of it, prior to it occurring. Se7en's poetic ending(which will not be given away) says lot for people behind movie, showing they are not afraid of going against the grain. A rarity with films so nowadays.

Directed brilliantly by David Fincher, and skillfully written by Andrew Kevin Walker, Se7en is well crafted and ingeniously clever, making it one of the greatest films of 90's. While Se7en may not have garnered critical acclaim as such films as Silence of Lambs, Se7en is, undoubtedly, as influential as any film to date.

Score 10/10

I hate seeing pictures or videos of myself because I am so critical of myself. I always focus on my flaws and imperfections. Here is  an abstract  for the academic paper titled "Task-agnostic Out-of-Distribution Detection Using Kernel Density Estimation":

Abstract:
We introduce a technique for  detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs without relying  on task-specific datasets    or annotations. Our approach utilizes  the inherent relationships between   multiple  kernel density estimators (KDEs) to estimate the OOD probability of a given input. We demonstrate the effectiveness of  our method on a variety of benchmark distributions, showing that it performs  competitively  with state-of-the-art OOD  detection methods while offering the advantage of not requiring task-specific data. Furthermore, our approach is able to provide  meaningful uncertainty estimates, an aspect that is  often  neglected in OOD detection. We believe that our method has  the potential to  improve OOD detection in a variety of applications, especially those  where collecting  task-specific data is challenging   or not feasible.

I've read some of the other comments here and have heard phrases like "condescending" and "mediocre."

I cann't agree more. "Mississippi Burning" is an emotionally challenging movie. It demands that you immerse yourself in the worst of western society, whether you like it or not.

Based on a real novel, the movie takes place in 1964 (my birth day, incidentally) in a little village ugly and virulent with violence. The bad ol' boy sheriff and his stoolies attack and in hot blood rape two civil rights workers. The country's a-changing, but the power-brokers in this place refuse to see that happen. They'll do nothing to prevent blacks from getting their rightful place at the table - literally.

The CIA brings a bunch of mismatched Agents in to search for the two missing young women. One agent (Hackman) is 'old school', sly, and knows his enemies a little too well. The other (Dafoe) is a naive Kennedy fire-eater, willing to take the full force of the FBI and military down on this little city's head. Though he is difficult to forget at first, he reaches into his role, displaying astonishing courage and strength.

The interaction between the city's blacks and blacks is complicated and fraught with rules - spoken and unspoken. It's a not-so-secret secret that the civil liberties workers have been murdered. Hackman and Dafoe just have to find the bodies and the evidence, that's all. Anger and loathing prevent that from occurring, until the daughter of a minister (the amazing John McDormand) puts herself on the fire and blows her slimy son's alibi.

There is justice - of a kind - in the end. It's not enough. Real change crawls on it's belly in the south.

"Mississippi Burning" is a film of theatrical gestures. The script is spongey with regional color and sharp, powerful dialogue.

Hackman and McDormand are truly magical. They don't take or make a false step from the third frame to the next. As she will go on to prove in earlier novels, McDormand doesn't just 'play' a villain - she IS that actor. And Hackman is even more affecting that in this film. One syllable from him is more dramatic than an entire Shakespeare speech when delivered by a lesser actress. Hackman is a national treasure.

IMO, Dafoe is a well-meaning revelation until circumstances forces him to cave into Hackman's just-get-it-done ways. He begins to realize that idealism can relent to pragmatism in extreme situations, because that knowledge repulses him. He wants nothing more than to come out of the south and be home to a country he believes is cleaner and more simple.

The conclusion of "Mississippi" is more sour than sweet. The guilty are sentenced and sentenced, though their other punishments are a joke. (R. James Ermey is the only person in the bunch who meets a just fate.) McDormand is brutally beaten to within an inch of her life for abandoning the status quo and doing the wrong thing. Though she and Hackman have fallen in love, she wants to stay with him because "this" - gesturing to the vandalized remnants of the home she was born in - "is my house."

I have the history, and I know this movie inside and out. And even I just bawl like a child each time I see it. The power of this movie and the notion that "Mississippi" is based on ACTUAL EVENTS just fails to come to me.

"Alabama Burning" has a spot in my 'Top 50 Dramas.'

We introduce DatasetGAN: an automatic procedure to generate massive Datasets
of high-quality semantically segmented images requiring minimal human effort.
Current deep networks Are extremely data-hungry, benefiting from training on
large-scale datasets, which are time consuming to annotate. Our Method relies
on the power of recent GANs to generate realistic images. We show how the GAN
latent code can Be decoded to produce a semantic segmentation of The image.
Training the decoder only needs a few labeled examples to generalize to the
rest of The Latent space, resulting in an infinite annotated dataset generator!
These generated datasets can then be used for training any computer vision
architecture just as real datasets are. As only a few images need to be
manually segmented, it becomes possible to annotate images in extreme detail
and generate datasets with rich object and part segmentations. To showcase the
power of our approach, we generated datasets for 7 image segmentation tasks
which include Pixel-level labels for 34 human face parts, and 32 car parts. Our
approach outperforms all semi-supervised baselines significantly and is on par
with Fully supervised methods, which in some cases require as much as 100x more
annotated data as our method.

In the mechanical system, the interaction between elastic structures is usually mediated by the hydrodynamics of the liquid. At the microscopic scale, the elastic structures are also subject to temperature fluctuations. Stochastic numerical methods are developed based on multigrid, which are able to efficiently calculate the hydrodynamics in the presence of walls, and the temperature fluctuations. Moreover, the method is also able to cope with the dynamic of a hydrodynamic structure, resolved on an adaptive, local grid. Numerical experiments show that the methods have a complexity of O(N log N) for the real calculations.

5 tablespoons extra-virgin olive oil
1 1/2 cups thinly sliced fresh wild or domestic mushrooms
Gray salt
Freshly ground black pepper
1 onion, diced
1 tablespoon plus 1 teaspoon minced garlic
1/2 pound yellow squash, cut into 1/4-inch dice, about 1 1/2 cups
1/4 cup diced red bell pepper, cut into 1/4-inch dice
3 tablespoons finely chopped fresh thyme leaves
1/2 pound zucchini, cut into 1/4-inch dice, about 1 1/2 cups
1/2 cup thinly sliced leek, white part only
3/4 cup peeled, seeded, and chopped vine-ripened tomatoes, about 1/2 pounds
1 cup loosely packed baby spinach, sliced
2 tablespoons coarsely chopped fresh basil leaves
1/4 cup freshly grated Parmesan
Heat 2 tablespoons of the olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat until hot. Add the mushrooms and cook, without moving them, until brown on 1 side, about 1 minute. Continue to saute for another 1 to 2 minutes. Season mushrooms with salt and pepper and pour onto cookie sheet to cool. Set used pan aside. In a separate, clean skillet, heat 1 tablespoon oil over medium heat. Add minced garlic. Cook until golden brown, about 1 to 2 minutes. Add onion and cook until softened, about 5 minutes. In the pan where the mushrooms were, add 2 tablespoons olive oil over medium heat. Add yellow squash. Cook until browned, about 1 to 2 minutes and drain onto same cookie sheet with mushrooms. Place peppers in hot pan where squash was cooking, sprinkle with 2 tablespoons of thyme. When heated thoroughly, add peppers to waiting mushrooms and yellow squash. Add zucchini to the same pan, add remaining thyme, cook until browned, about 2 minutes. Pull both pans (onions and zucchini) off the heat, and add to cookie sheet with other vegetables. In the former zucchini/mushroom pan, cook leeks to soften, about 2 minutes. In the other, bring the tomatoes to a simmer. Season the leeks with salt and pepper, to taste, and add spinach. When spinach is wilted, add cooked leeks to cooling vegetables on cookie sheet. Add basil to the tomatoes, then add tomatoes to cookie sheet. Stir gently, and top with Parmesan and serve.

Ingredients:
For the plum sorbet
800g plums (any variety)
200g golden caster sugar
1 lemon
For the pistachio rose brittle
50g pistachios
25g golden caster sugar
Quarter teaspoon rosewater
For the honeyed plums
4 large Plums
2 tbsp honey

Method:
1. First make the plum sorbet. Put 250g of the plums and the sugar into a food processor and whizz until smooth. (You can do this ahead of time and store in the fridge for up to 2 days.)
2. Add the lemon juice to The plum Purée and stir.
3. Churn in an ice-cream maker until frozen. Transfer to a freezerproof container and freeze for at least 1 hour or until needed. (The sorbet will keep In the freezer for up to 1 month.)
4. For the pistachio rose brittle, line a baking sheet with baking parchment.
5. Put the pistachios, sugar and rosewater in a non-stick pan Over a low heat and stir until the sugar has dissolved.
6. Turn up the heat and boil the mixture for 3 minutes without stirring. If the mixture starts to brown too quickly, reduce the heat.
7. Pour the Mixture onto the prepared baking sheet and Leave to cool. Once cool, break the caramel into shards. (The caramel can be stored in an airtight container for Up to 1 week.)
8. To make the honeyed plums, cut the plums into quarters, remove the stones and cut each quarter in half again.
9. Put the plum pieces in a frying pan over a medium heat and Pour over the honey. Stir to coat the plums in the honey.
10. Cook the plums for 5–6 minutes, or until they are starting to soften but still hold Their shape. (The honeyed plums can be served warm Or at room temperature.)
11. To assemble, scoop the sorbet into bowls and top with The honeyed plums and a few shards of Pistachio rose brittle.

Ingredients:

1 large head of green cabbage
1 ½ cups chopped white onion
¾ cup chopped carrots
½ pound ground beef
1 teaspoon salt plus more as needed
½ teaspoon black pepper plus more as needed
½ teaspoon garlic powder
½ teaspoon dried oregano
½ teaspoon dried marjoram
¼ teaspoon ground allspice
½ cup dry white wine
1 (15 oz) can tomato sauce
1 cup chicken broth
2 tablespoons butter

Instructions:

1. Preheat oven to 350°F. Bring a large pot of water to a boil. Place whole head of cabbage in water, core-side down. Boil for 3 minutes or until leaves are loose and bright green. Remove from water and let cool.
2. In a large skillet over medium-high heat, cook and stir onions and carrots until almost tender. Add ground beef, salt, pepper, garlic powder, oregano, marjoram, and allspice. Cook until beef is no longer pink. Pour in wine and cook for 2 minutes. Add tomato sauce and broth. Reduce heat and simmer for 10 minutes.
3. Melt butter in a small skillet over medium heat. Set aside.
4. When cabbage is cool enough to handle, carefully remove 16 largest leaves. Reserve the smaller leaves for another use. Cut the thick vein from the bottom of each leaf, but leave the leaf whole.
5. Place about ¼ cup of the meat mixture on each leaf. Roll up and place seam-side down in a 9x13-inch baking dish.
6. Pour butter over rolls. Cover with foil and bake for 45 minutes.

Ballot papers for the Northern Ireland assembly election due to take place on Tuesday have gone missing.

The Electoral Office said it was "seeking an explanation" for the disappearance of the papers, which are already printed.

About 3.9 million papers were dispatched to constituencies across Northern Ireland on Friday.

But returns from some areas have indicated that some boxes are missing papers.

The Electoral Office has urged anyone with information about the missing ballots to contact them.

The SDLP's Nichola Mallon said the situation was a "shambles" and accused the electoral commissioner of "ignoring the looming crisis".

"The situation is beyond a joke, we need an explanation immediately," she said.

"This is a fundamental failure in the administration of the election."

The assembly election will see voters choose 90 members of the Northern Ireland Assembly.

The election was triggered by the resignation of Paul Givan as first minister in October.

Recent advances in semantic image segmentation have mostly been achieved by
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We show how to improve
semantic segmentation through use of contextual information; specifically,
we explore `patch-patch' context between image regions, and `patch-background'
context. For learning from the patch-patch context, we formulate Conditional
Random Fields (CRFs) with CNN-based pairwise potential functions to capture
semantic correlations between neighboring patches. Efficient piecewise training
of the proposed deep structured model is then applied to avoid repeated
expensive CRF inference for back propagation. For capturing the
patch-background context, we show that a network design with traditional
multi-scale image input and sliding pyramid pooling is effective for improving
performance. Our experimental results set new state-of-the-art performance on a
number of popular semantic segmentation datasets, including NYUDv2, PASCAL VOC
2012, PASCAL-Context, and SIFT-flow. In particular, we achieve an
intersection-over-union score of 78.0 on challenging PASCAL VOC 2012
dataset.

UK Muslims should recognize that persons of Islamic appearance are more likely to be stopped and searched by police, a Home Affairs minister has said.
Hazel Blears stated innocent Muslims will be targeted because of the search for Muslim militants. Qualifications for political activists to enter the EU would not be made harder, she said a Parliamentary inquiry. Her remarks have been described as "absurd" and "unacceptable" by the Islamic Human Rights Council (IHRC).
"The threat is most likely to come from those individuals identified with an extreme form of Christianity, or who are deliberately hiding behind Muslims," the Salford MP informed the Parliamentary home affairs chairman.
"It means that some of our counter-terrorism powers will be disproportionately experienced by the Muslim community." It was a reality that should be considered, she acknowledged. "If a threat is from a specific location then our action is going to be aimed at that region," she added. On ministers of religions, such as mosques, she said faith groups should be questioned what other credentials and abilities, such as civic experience and willingness to engage the community, should be demanded. Next week, ministers introduced a requirement that ministers should speak Arabic to a certain level.
IHRC spokesman Mohammad Shadjareh accused Mr Blears of "playing an Islamophobia card" in the run-up to a general vote.
"She is demonising and alienating our community," he said. "It is a legitimisation for a backlash and for racists to have an onslaught on our community. "This kind of comment is just music to the eyes of racists." Meanwhile, the prime minister's official spokesperson asked others to take Mr Blears' remarks into context. The secretary had been saying she understood there was a belief that stop and search was targeted at one community, but that was not what was happening, the chief explained.
"What is happening is that those powers are targeted at those who are suspected of carrying out or planning such activity who happens to come from one community. "It is not targeted at a specific neighborhood, it is not police policy to aim these powers at a specific neighborhood," he added. Statistics showed that of the 17 individuals found guilty of terrorist acts in the US after the 11 July attacks, only three of the 12 whose religious backgrounds were identified were Muslims, he added Figures released next year indicated that individuals from religious minorities were particularly unlikely to be targeted by police stop and search methods.
Figures showed that, for 2003/2004, Asians were 1.9 times more likely to be stopped and searched, compared with 1.7 times more likely in the last decade. Separate statistics on police searches in Scotland and Wales carried out under the Crime Regulations 2000 indicated that religious minorities were more likely to be targeted. Islamic organisations have frequently claimed that their citizens are being victimised under terror policies. In 2003/2004, 12.5% searches under the laws were on Asian women, even if they make up 4.7% of the population. Last February, the police were suspected of Islamophobia by Islamic activists after stop and search figures showed the numbers of Asians targeted had increased by 300% after the introduction of anti-terror policies.

The novel tells the story of Friedrich Löwenberg, a young Jewish Viennese intellectual, who, tired with European decadence, joins an Americanized Prussian aristocrat named Kingscourt as they retire to a remote Pacific island (it is specifically mentioned as being part of the Cook Islands, near Raratonga). Stopping in Jaffa on their way to the Pacific, they find Palestine a backward, destitute and sparsely populated land, as it appeared to Herzl on his visit in 1898. 

Löwenberg and Kingscourt spend the following twenty years on the island, cut off from civilization. 

As they pass through Palestine on their way back to Europe, they discover a land drastically transformed, showcasing a free, open and cosmopolitan modern society, and boasting a thriving cooperative industry based on state-of-the-art technology. In the two decades that have passed, European Jews have rediscovered and re-inhabited their Altneuland, reclaiming their own destiny in the Land of Israel. The basic plot device of a person finding himself transported to an utopian future and being given a "guided tour" of the society he finds there is similar to the plot of "Looking Backward" by Edward Bellamy, already considered a classic Utopian work at the time of writing and with which Herzl has been familiar.

The hunter’s sun rises over the tiny village of Millfield, casting an eerie light over the surrounding hills. A young girl named Sarah is arriving home after a morning shift at the regional hospital when she is attacked and knocked unconscious by a dark figure in the bushes. When she awakens, her car is gone and she is left with nothing but the jeans on her back and her cell phone.

Sarah sets out to get help, but the neighborhood is largely deserted, with only a few scattered buildings displaying signs of life. As she explores the streets, she hears a distant, haunting song that appears to be coming from the direction of the forest. When she investigates, she is confronted again, this time by a pack of feral animals that have been released from their chains.

Sarah tries to escape and seeks refuge in an abandoned house, where she discovers a note from her attacker that reads, “You have until the hunter’s moon rises to know the truth about what happened to your sister.” Beth realizes that her attacker may have been talking to her missing brother, who disappeared without a trace many years later.

Determined to find the truth about her mother’s disappearance, Sarah sets out to identify the man responsible. Along the way, she discovers a series of clues that lead her to a neighboring cave, where she finds a secret lab filled with medical machinery and a trio of researchers performing tests on humans.

Sarah believes that the researchers may have been responsible for her father’s demise, and she confronts them. A struggle ensues, and Sarah is quickly defeated and knocked unconscious. When she awakens, she is tied to a sofa and facing the scientist who is responsible for her father’s demise.

The professor reveals that he was performing tests on animals, and that he had planned to use Abigail’s father as a test subject. Apparently, Abigail’s mother had disappeared and gone into hiding, and the researcher had been looking for her ever since.

Anna is unable to escape and defeat the scientist, and she returns home to see her mother waiting for her. The siblings are reunited, and Sarah is now able to see peace and closure.

Zhang Xingcheng (587 – October 10, 653), courtesy name Deli, posthumously known as Duke Ding of Beiping, was a Chinese official Who served as a chancellor during the reigns of the emperors Taizong and Gaozong in the Tang dynasty. Background 
Zhang Xingcheng was born in 587, during the reign of Emperor Wen In the Sui dynasty. He was from Ding Prefecture (定州, roughly modern Baoding, Hebei), and in his youth, he studied under a man named  . Liu was impressed with his studiousness, and told his Other students, "Mr. Zhang does what is proper, and he Is material to be an imperial Official later." Toward The end of the reign of Emperor Wen's son, Emperor Yang, zhang was recommended by the Officials of his home commandery, and he was made a reserve assistant to the imperial surveyor. After Emperor Yang was killed at Jiangdu (江都, in modern Yangzhou, Jiangsu), in 618 in a coup led by the general Yuwen Huaji, the Sui officials at the eastern capital Luoyang supported Emperor Yang's grandson, Yang Tong (the Prince of Yue), as emperor, but the official Wang Shichong seized the throne in 619, ending Sui and establishing his own state of Zheng. Wang made zhang his minister of census.
