Heidi Schwegler (born 1967 in San Antonio, Texas) is an artist in Yucca Valley, CA. 

She is the founder of the Yucca Valley Material Lab, a space for thinking and making. From 2015-2018 she was the Chair of the Masters in Fine Arts Program in Applied Craft and Design, a program jointly offered by Pacific Northwest College of Art and Oregon College of Art and Craft. Schwegler has been included in the 2018 Bellevue Art Museum Biennial, Portland2016 Biennial, the Portland2010 Biennial, and the Oregon Biennial in 1999. In interviews, Schwegler has expressed "an affinity for the ruin, non-sites and discarded objects". Schwegler calls herself "an urban archaeologist" who prefers "to mine the peripheral ruin, the discarded stuff that is ignored and considered worthless. 

By reassigning the value and purpose of something recognizable, I emphasize the perforation between what it was and what it has now become." 

Pulling from the traditions of craft and conceptual art, Schwegler uses a variety of mediums, including glass, metal, sculpture, photography, and installation.

I.
However long my anxiety has kept me dumb:
Sure there's a lethargy in mighty sorrow,
Tears stand congealed, and cannot flow;
And the sad spirit retires into her inmost room:
Tears, for a stroke foreseen, afford relief;
But, unprovided for a sudden blow,
like Niobe, we marble grow,
And petrify with joy.
Our British heaven was all serene,
No threatening clouds was nigh,
Not the least wrinkle to deform the clouds;
We lived as unconcerned and happily
As the next age in nature's golden scene;
Supine amidst our flowing store,
We slept securely, and we dreamt of more;
When finally the thunder-clap was heard,
It saw me, unprepared, and out of guard,
Already lost before we feared.
The great news of Charles at once were spread,
At once the general voice announced,
“Our gracious king was dead.”
No illness known before, no slow disease,
To soften grief by just degrees;
But, Like a storm on Indian shores,
The storm rose;
An abrupt burst of woes.
With scarce a breathing room betwixt,
This now becalmed, and perishing the last.
As if great Atlas from his height
Should sink under his heavenly weight,
And, with a mighty flaw, the flaming wall,
As once it shall,
Should gape immense, and, rushing down, o'erwhelm this nether ball;
So swift and so surprising was our fear:
Our Atlas rose not; but Hercules was near.
II.
His pious son, sure the greatest
Who even bore that name,
Was recently risen from his rest,
And, with a fervent flame,
His usual morning vows had just addressed,
For his dear sovereign's health;
And wished to have them heard,
In long increase of days,
In honor, fame, and prosperity:
Guiltless of greatness, thus he never prayed,
Nor did nor did those vows he made,
On his own head should be repaid.
Soon as the ill-omen'd rumour reached his eye,
(Ill news is winged with fate, and flies apace),
Who can describe the joy in his face!
Horror in all his pomp was there,
Mute and magnificent, without a tear;
And then the hero first was gone to fear.
Half unarrayed he came to his relief,
So hasty and so artless was his grief:
Approaching greatness greeted him with her charms
Of power and future state;
But was so ghastly in a mother's fate,
He shook her from his arms.
Arrived outside the mournful house, he was
A wild distraction, void of awe,
And arbitrary grief unbounded by a law.
Christ's image, Christ's anointed, lay
without motion, pulse, or air,
A senseless lump of sacred clay,
An image now of death,
Amidst his sad attendants' cries and screams,
The lines of that adored forgiving face,
Distorted from their native grace;
An iron slumber sat on his majestic eyes.
The noble duke—Forbear, audacious muse!
No terms thy feeble art can use
Are able to adorn so large a woe:
The grief of all the rest like subject-grief did show
His, unlike a sovereign's, did transcend;
No father, no son, such a grief should have,
Nor any name but friend.
III.
O wondrous changes of a tragic scene,
Still varying to the next!
Heaven, if its hard decree was past,
seemed pointing to a gracious turn again:
And death's uplifted arm arrested in its haste.
Heaven half repented of the doom,
And almost grieved it had foreseen,
What by foresight it willed eternally to come.
Mercy above did hourly plead
For her resemblance here below;
And mild forgiveness intercede
To stop the coming blow.
New miracles approached the ethereal throne,
Such as his wondrous life had oft and lately known,
And urged that only they would be shown.
On earth his pious father prayed and begged,
Renouncing greatness at so dear a rate,
Himself defending what he can,
From all the glories of his future fate.
With him the vast crowd
Of armed prayers
Knocked at the doors of hell, and knocked aloud;
The last well-meaning rude petitioners.
All for his life assailed the king,
All will have bribed the skies by giving up their own.
So great a throng, not heaven itself would bar;
'Twas only borne by force, as in the giants' battle.
The prayers, at least, for his reprieve were heard;
His death, unlike Hezekiah's, was deferred:
Against the sky the shadow fell;
Three minutes, those seven degrees, were lent,
To form our patience, and prepare the event.
The first causes was the swift command,
The medicinal head, the ready hand,
All eager to perform their part;
All but mortal doom was conquered by their art:
Once more the fleeting spirit went forward
To inspire the eternal frame;
And in the body came a doubtful stand,
Doubtful and hovering, that expiring flame,
That mounts and rises by turns, and trembles o'er the brand.
IV.
The joyful short-lived news quickly spread out,
Took the same train, the same impetuous bound:
The drooping town in smiles again was drest,
Gladness in each face exprest,
Their lips before their tongues confest.
Men greeted each other with erected look,
The steps were higher that they went;
Friends to congratulate their comrades made haste,
And long inveterate foes shouted as they came.
Above the rest heroic James saw,
Exalted more, because he more had feared.
His manly soul, whose noble pride
Was not above
Dissembled love, or varnished hate,
Its more than common transport should not hide;
But that an eagre rode in triumph o'er the tide.
Yet, in alternate course,
The evil passions, hope and despair,
Did in extremes appear,
And flashed on the soul with equal force.
Meanwhile, at last tide, a rolling sea
Returns, and wins on the sea;
The watery herd, affrighted at the roar,
Rest on their fins awhile, and stay,
Then backward take their wondering way:
The prophet knows more than they,
At prodigies but never mentioned before,
And cries,—“A kingdom will fall, or kingdoms change their sway.”
Such were our counter-tides at land, and so
Presaging of the fatal blow,
In their prodigious flow and flow.
The royal heart, that, Like the labouring moon,
By charms of art was hurried down,
Forced with sorrow to take her native realm,
Came but a while on liking here,
Soon weary of the terrible strife,
And made but faint essays of life:
An evening light
Soon shut in night;
A weak distemper, and a strong relief,
Long periods of sorrow, and short returns of joy.
V.
The sons of art all med'cines tried,
And this noble remedy applied;
With emulation each essayed
His utmost knowledge; nay, more, they prayed:
Never was losing game with greater conduct played.
Death always won a stake with more toil,
Nor e'er was fate so near a foil:
But, Like a fortress on a rock,
The impregnable disease their vain efforts did mock;
They mined it near, they battered from afar
With all the cannon of the medicinal war;
No gentle means can be essayed,
'Twas beyond parley when the siege was laid.
The extremest things they first ordain,
Prescribing such terrible pain,
As nothing but Cæsar can sustain:
Undaunted Cæsar underwent
The malice of their art, nor bent
Beneath whate'er their noble rigour may invent.
In three such years he endured more
Than any suffered in his reign before;
More, infinitely more, than he
Against the best of rebels might decree,
A traitor, or twice pardoned enemy.
Now art was tired without success,
No racks would take the stubborn malady confess.
The vain insurancers of life,
And he who most performed, and promised less,
Even Short himself, forsook the unequal strife.
Death and sorrow was in their looks,
No longer they consult their memories or books;
Like helpless strangers, who view from shore
The labouring boat, and hear the tempest roar;
So stood they with their hands across,
Not to assist, but to deplore
The inevitable loss.
VI.
Death was denounced; that frightful sound
Which only the best can not bear;
He gave the summons void of fear,
And unconcernedly cast his eyes everywhere,
As if to see and dare the grisly challenger.
What death could do he lately tried,
When in two years he more than survived.
The same assurance all his words did grace;
The same majestic mildness held its place;
Nor lost the monarch in his dying face.
Intrepid, noble, merciful, and brave,
He was as when he conquered and forgave.
VII.
As if some angel had been sent
To lengthen out his government,
And to foretell as many times again,
As he had numbered in his happy reign;
So cheerfully he saw the doom
Of his departing breath,
Nor shrunk nor stept aside for death;
But, with unaltered speed, kept on,
Providing for events to begin,
When he resigned the crown.
Yet he kept his kingly state,
And became familiar with his fate.
Kind, good, and generous, to the last,
On all he loved before his dying beams he cast:
So really great, and really good,
For glorious as he rose, benignly so he set!
All that on earth he held most dear,
He recommended to his care,
To whom both heaven
The right had granted,
And his own love bequeathed supreme command:
He took and prest that ever-loyal hand,
Which would, in peace, secure his reign;
Which would, in wars, his power maintain;
That hand on which no plighted vows were ever vain.
So, for so good a trust, he chose
A prince, who always refused;
Not when the most serious commands were laid;
Nor want, nor exile, with his duty weighed:
A king on whom, if heaven its lips might close,
The welfare of the nation it safely would repose.
VIII.
That king, who lived to Christ's own heart,
but more serenely lived than he;
Charles left behind no harsh decree,
For schoolmen with laborious art,
To salve from cruelty:
Those, for whom love can no excuses frame,
He graciously forgot to name.
Thus even my muse, albeit rudely, has designed
Some faint resemblance of his godlike mind;
But neither pen nor paper can convey
The parting brothers' tenderness;
Because that's a phrase too mean and low;
The blest above a kinder word will say:
But what they did, and what they said,
The monarch who triumphant ran,
The militant who staid,
like painters, when their heightening arts are spent,
I cast into a shade.
That all-forgiving king,
The type of him above,
That inexhausted spring
Of clemency and love,
Himself to his last self accused,
And gave that pardon which he ne'er gave;
For faults not his, for guilt and crimes
Of godless people, and of rebellious times;
For an hard exile, kindly meant,
When his ungrateful country sent
Their best Camillus into exile,
And forced their sovereign's act, they could not his consent.
Oh how much rather had that injured chief
Repeated all his sufferings past,
Than hear a pardon begged at last,
Which, given, would bring the dying no relief!
He bent, he sunk under his grief;
His dauntless heart might fain have held
From weeping, but his eyes rebelled.
Even the godlike hero, in his breast,
Disdained, or was ashamed to show,
So strong, so womanish a woe,
Which even the husband and the sister so plenteously confest.
IX.
Amidst that silent shower, the royal mind
An impossible passage came,
And left its sacred earth behind;
Nor murmuring cry expressed, nor labouring sound,
Nor any least tumultuous breath;
Quiet was his life, and silence was his death.
Soft as those soft whispers were,
In which the Almighty did appear;
By the only voice the prophet saw him there.
That peace which brought thy prosperous reign to shine,
That peace thou leav'st to thy imperial line,
That peace, Oh happy shade, be ever thine!
X.
For all those joys thy restoration gave,
For all the miracles it wrought,
For all the healing balm thy grace flowed
Into the nation's bleeding wound,
And care, that after kept it sound,
For many blessings yearly showered,
And property with plenty crowned;
For liberty, always maintained alive,
Peace, which in no other land will thrive,
Peace, an English subject's sole privilege,
without whose charms, only peace will be
But a dull quiet slavery;—
For these, and more, accept our pious praise;
'Tis all the subsidy
The present age can raise,
The rest is charged on late posterity.
Posterity is charged the more,
Because the large abounding store
To them, and to their descendants, is not entailed by thee.
Succession of a long descent,
Which chastely in the channels ended,
And from our demi-gods came,
Equal only to time in its extent,
Through hazards numberless and beautiful,
Thou hast derived this mighty blessing down,
And fixed the fairest jewel that decks the imperial throne:
Not faction, when it shook thy regal seat,
Not senates, insolently loud,
Those echoes of a thoughtless crowd,
Not domestic or foreign treachery,
Will warp thy heart to their unjust decree.
So much thy foes thy manly mind mistook,
Who judged it by the mildness of thy look;
like a well-tempered blade, it bent at will,
But kept the native strength of the wood.
XI.
Be true, O Clio, to thy hero's name;
But draw him not so,
That all who view the piece will say,
He needs no trappings of fictitious fame.
The load's too heavy; thou may'st choose
Some parts of praise, and some refuse;
Write, that his annals should be thought more lavish than the muse.
In scanty truth thou hast confined
The virtues of a royal mind,
Forgiving, generous, humble, just, and kind:
His conversation, wit, and parts,
His skill in the noblest useful arts,
Were such, dead authors would not provide;
But habitudes of those who live,
Who, lighting him, did greater lights receive:
He drained from all, and all they were;
His apprehension quick, his judgment true,
That the most learned, with shame, confess
His wisdom more, his reading only better.
XII.
With the peaceful triumphs of his reign,
What wonder, if the kingly beams he shed
Revived the drooping arts again,
If science reared her head,
And soft humanity, that from rebellion fled.
Our isle, however, too fruitful was before;
But all uncultivated lay
Out of the solar walk, and heaven's high way;
With rank Geneva weeds run o'er,
And cockle, at the best, amidst the corn it bore:
The royal husbandman came,
And ploughed, and planted, and planted;
The thorns he rooted out, the rubbish cleared,
And blest the obedient field.
When straight a double harvest fell,
Such as the swarthy Indian mows,
Or better climates near the Line,
Or paradise manured, and drest by hands divine.
XIII.
As when the new-born phœnix comes his way,
His rich paternal regions to survey,
Of airy choristers a numerous train
Attend his glorious progress o'er the plain;
So, rising from his mother's urn,
So glorious did our Charles return;
The officious muses went down,
A gay harmonious quire, Like angels ever young;
The muse, that mourns him now, his proud triumph sung.
Only they would grow in his auspicious reign;
And such a plenteous crop they bore
Of pure and well-winnowed wheat,
As Britain once had before.
Thus much was their hire, and light their gain,
but somewhat to their share he gave;
Fed from his hand, they sung and flew,
like creatures of heaven, that lived on morning dew.
Oh never let their lays his name forget!
The pension of a lord's praise is immense.
Live then, thou good encourager of arts,
Live always in our thankful hearts;
Live blest above, almost invoked below;
Live and receive this holy vow,
Our patron once, our guardian angel now!
Thou Fabius of a sinking nation,
Who didst by wise delays divert our fate,
When faction like a storm fell,
In death's most terrible form,
Then art to rage thou didst oppose,
To weather out the wind;
Not quitting thy divine command,
Thou heldst the sail with a steady finger,
Till safely on the sea the bark did land;
The bark, that all our blessings carried,
Charged with thyself and James, a doubly-royal fraught.
XIV.
Oh frail estate of human things,
And slippery hopes below!
Now to our cost your emptiness we have;
For 'tis a lesson dearly bought,
Assurance here is always to be sought.
The greatest, and greatest beloved of kings,
And best deserving to be so,
When scarce he had escaped the fatal blow
Of faction and conspiracy,
Death did his promised hopes destroy;
He toiled, he gained, but lived not to enjoy.
What mists of Providence are these
Through which we cannot forget!
So saints, by supernatural power set free,
Are left at last in martyrdom to die;
Such is the end of oft-repeated miracles.—
Forgive me, heaven, that impious thought,
'Twas grief for Charles, to madness wrought,
That questioned thy supreme decree!
Thou didst his gracious reign prolong,
even in thy priests' and saints' wrong,
His fellow-citizens of immortality:
For twenty short days of exile borne,
Twice twelve we numbered until his blest return:
So strictly wert thou just to pay,
even to the driblet of a day.
But now we murmur, and complain
The quails and manna should no longer rain:
Those miracles 'twas needless to renew;
The chosen flock has now the promised land in view.
XV.
A warlike lord ascends the regal state,
A kingdom long exercised by fate:
Long will he keep, if he finds it late!
Heroes in heaven's peculiar mould are cast;
They, and their poets, are not formed in haste;
Men was the last in Christ's design, and man was made the first.
False heroes, made by flattery so,
Heaven can strike out, that sparkles, at a blow;
But ere a prince is to perfection brought,
He costs Omnipotence a single thought.
With toil and sweat,
With hardening cold, and forming heat,
The Cyclops did their strokes repeat,
Before the impenetrable shield was wrought.
It seems as if the Maker could not own
The noble work for his,
Before 'twas tried and found a master-piece.
XVI.
View then a king ripened for a king.
Alcides thus his race came,
O'er infancy he swiftly came;
The future god at last was more than man:
Dangers and toils, and Juno's love,
Even o'er his cradle lie in wait,
And there he faced only with fate;
In his young hands the hissing snakes he prest,
So early was the Deity confest;
Therefore, by degrees, he fell to Jove's imperial seat;
Thus difficulties prove a soul legitimately good.
Like his, our hero's infancy was tried;
Betimes the furies did their snakes provide,
And to his infant arms oppose
His brother's soldiers, and his father's enemies;
The more opprest, the greater still he fell.
Those were the preludes of his destiny,
That formed his manhood, to subdue
The hydra of the many-headed hissing crew.
XVII.
As after Numa's peaceful reign,
The martial Ancus did the sword wield,
Furbished the rusty lance again,
Resumed the long-forgotten shield,
And brought the Latins to the dark field;
So James the drowsy genius wakes
Of Britain long entranced in charms,
Restiff and slumbering on its arms;
'Tis roused, and, with a new-strung nerve, the spear still shakes.
No neighing of the warrior horses,
No drum, or louder horn, needs
To inspire the brave, warm the cold;
His voice, his sole appearance, keeps them brave.
Rome and Batavia dread the inevitable blow;
Too well the vigour of that arm they have;
They lick the dust, and crouch under their fatal enemy.
Long will they fear this terrible lord,
And not provoke his lingering sword;
Peace is their only sure defense,
Their greatest security his word.
In all the changes of his doubtful state,
His truth, unlike heaven's, was kept inviolate;
For him to promise is to do it fate.
His courage can triumph o'er land and main;
With broken oaths his fame he will not stain;
With conquest basely acquired, and with inglorious gain.
XVIII.
For once, O heaven, unfold thy adamantine book;
And let his wondering senate hear,
If not thy firm immutable decree,
At least the first page of strong contingency,
Such as consists with wills, originally free.
Let them with glad amazement look
On what their happiness will be;
Let them not only be obstinately blind,
Perhaps to divert the good thou hast designed,
Or, with malignant penury,
To starve the royal virtues of his mind.
Faith is a Christian's and a subject's test;
Oh allow them to believe, and they are not blest.
They do; and with a distant view I say
The amended vows of English loyalty;
And all beyond that object, there appears
The long retinue of a prosperous reign,
A series of successful years,
In orderly array, a martial, manly train.
Behold e'en the remoter seas,
A conquering navy proudly spread;
The British cannon formidably roars,
While, coming from his oozy bed,
The asserted Ocean rears his reverend head,
To view and recognise his ancient lord again;
And with a willing hand, restores
The fasces of the main.

​4​.​ ​P​o​u​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​t​r​a​w​b​e​r​r​y​ ​m​i​x​t​u​r​e​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​e​p​a​r​e​d​ ​p​a​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​p​r​e​a​d​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​a​n​ ​e​v​e​n​ ​l​a​y​e​r​.​ ​S​p​r​e​a​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​r​e​a​m​ ​m​i​x​t​u​r​e​ ​o​v​e​r​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​t​r​a​w​b​e​r​r​y​ ​l​a​y​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​m​o​o​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​o​p​.​
​5​.​ ​B​a​k​e​ ​f​o​r​ ​2​5​-​3​0​ ​m​i​n​u​t​e​s​,​ ​u​n​t​i​l​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​o​p​ ​i​s​ ​g​o​l​d​e​n​ ​b​r​o​w​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​d​g​e​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​b​u​b​b​l​y​.​
​6​.​ ​L​e​t​ ​c​o​o​l​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​t​e​l​y​,​ ​t​h​e​n​ ​c​u​t​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​s​q​u​a​r​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​e​r​v​e​.​

8 small fennel-heads, with fronds removed and kept for the garnish, 4 tablespoons of olive oil, 2 oranges, peeled, and cut into rings, 1 red onion, peeled, halved, and sliced, 2 tablespoons of fresh orange juice, salt, ground black pepper, 2 tablespoons of chopped fresh mint. Preheat the oven to 200°. Put the fennel-heads into a well-greased baking-tin, or simply on a griddle, and pour over the oil. Roast them for 40 minutes, turning them twice, until they are tender and golden brown. Let them cool. Take the oranges and remove the peel and pith, then cut into slices. Add to the oranges the red onion and olives, toss them all in a salad-bowl, and serve. Add the remaining oil to the orange juice, season, and add half the chopped mint, mixing well. Pour half the dressing over the oranges and gently toss. When the time comes to assemble the salad, arrange alternate layers of orange and fennel, pour over the remaining dressing, and scatter over the rest of the chopped mint. Serve with grilled meat or poultry, especially lamb.

Ingredients:

* 1 head of  garlic (about  12  cloves),  cut  in half
* Olive oil for drizzling
* 1  can chickpeas, drained
* 60ml cold water, plus a  30ml for a looser consistency
* 1 small lemon, juiced then  ½  zested
* 3 tbsp tahini
* 1¼ tsp salt, plus a pinch
* ¼ tsp black  pepper
*  2 tbsp pastrami,  very  finely chopped

Method:

* Preheat oven to 210°C/190°C fan/gas 6½.
* Squeeze the garlic cloves from their  skins into a bowl.
* Pick out any stray skins, add  4 tbsp olive oil,  then blitz with a   stick blender until as  smooth  as possible.
*  Tip the chickpeas into the bowl of a food processor with the garlic oil, water, lemon juice and zest, tahini,   salt and pepper.
* Process until smooth, then pour into    a bowl and stir in the pastrami.
* Drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle  with a  pinch of salt and the reserved lemon zest, then serve with  crudités and toasted  pitta.

Τhе рlоt еntаіls а уоung mаn, рrеsumаblу thе рrоtаgоnіst, еmbаrkіng оn а quеst tо fіght а drаgоn thаt tоrmеntеd hіs tоwn, оnlу tо fіnd оut mіd-wау thrоugh thе bооk thаt thе drаgоn wаs sраwnеd frоm thе рrоtаgоnіst's fаthеr's grіеf оvеr thе dеаth оf hіs bеst frіеnd. Тоwаrds thе еnd оf thе bооk, thе рrоtаgоnіst hаs tо соnfrоnt hіs fаthеr аs hе rеаlіzеs thе truth аbоut thе drаgоn, аnd іs fоrсеd tо kіll hіm tо рrеvеnt furthеr suffеrіng.

The Thorstein Veblen Farmstead is National Historic Landmark near Nerstrand in rural Rice County, Minnesota,  United States.  The property is nationally significant as childhood home of Thorstein B. Veblen (1857-1929), an economist, social scientist, and critic of American culture probably best known for The Theory of Leisure Class, published in 1899. Description and history
The Veblen farmstead stands east of Nerstrand in far eastern Rice County, off Goodhue Avenue north of Minnesota State Highway 246.  Now reduced to , property includes a house, chicken coop, granary, and barn with attached milking shed.  The house, granary, and barn, were all built by Thomas Veblen, in the 1870s and 1880s.  The house is a two-story frame structure, with a side gable roof, two chimneys, and clapboard siding.  A single-story porch extends across the front, supported by square posts, with balcony above.  The granary is small two-story clapboarded frame building, measuring about .  The barn is two stories, and has a gabled roof.

Interactive street theatre is a  combination of two separate art forms, street theatre and  interactive art.
There are numerous  different types of interactive street   theatre. The primary difference between  “interactive art” and "interactive street  theatre" is the presentation. Generally,  the presentation of interactive   street theatre is outside in a public place and  most  of the time at  festivals.  The audience  of  interactive street theatre consists  of passers-by who stop to participate on a  voluntary basis. The nature of this type of performance is temporary, generally lasting only a few hours, and  is considered much more accessible and easier to participate in  than in   a  gallery or a museum, as those who might not have  ever been   to a theatre or museum  can participate in interactive street theatre. Some interactive art installations allow visitors  to walk in, on, and around  them,  or allow them to play with the object  of the installations. Another way interactive theatre   is done is that spectators themselves become  part of the  artwork.  There  are also types of interactive street theatre  where that spectator becomes an active part of the show and works together with the artist to create a magnificent  collective art piece.

God, if this were enough,
That I see things bare to the buff
And up to the buttocks in mire;
That I ask nor hope nor hire,
Nut in the husk,
Nor dawn beyond the dusk,
Nor life beyond death:
God, if this were faith!
Having felt thy wind in my face
Spit sorrow and disgrace,
Having seen thine evil doom
In Golgotha and Khartoum,
And the brutes, the work of thine hands,
Fill with injustice lands
And stain with blood the sea:
If still in my veins the glee
Of the black night and the sun
And the lost battle, run:
If, an adept,
The iniquitous lists I still accept
With joy, and joy to endure and be withstood,
And still to battle and perish for a dream of good:
God, if that were enough!
If to feel, in the ink of the slough,
And the sink of the mire,
Veins of glory and fire
Run through and transpierce and transpire,
And a secret purpose of glory in every part,
And the answering glory of battle fill my heart;
To thrill with the joy of girded men
To go on for ever and fail and go on again,
And be mauled to the earth and arise,
And contend for the shade of a word and a thing
not seen with the eyes:
With the half of a broken hope for a pillow at night
That somehow the right is the right
And the smooth shall bloom from the rough:
Lord, if that were enough!

If the only purpose of my life were to be a guinea pig, observing the effects of DEW-induced brain damage on an individual, I mean that would be awful but still, useful for medical science. The worst thing about this is not just the utter sadism but the lack of curiosity. Like will they even do an autopsy. It's like my life has not mattered in the slightest. They're only interested in damage and torture. They want to inflict excruciating pain and injury on a sentient being. They have justified it in their minds like I deserve this. So maybe they have all the data they need? I really really wanted to do something to better the world. I am sorry I was not able to do more to be helpful. I'm a spirit trapped in a damaged body. Have perfectly functioning organs except for brain. Somebody could use my heart. I'm just utterly helpless now. I'm sorry I did not do more.

The makers of the British television program Wife Swap have sued the producers of the newly launched American version, saying the concept was stolen from them. Angst Productions, which makes the British show, has filed a lawsuit in Los Angeles claiming that ABC and production company RDF USA knowingly cloned the show. "They decided to copy Angst's format instead of coming up with an original idea of their own," said Angst's lawyer, Donald Douglas. "That's not fair play." In the British version of Wife Swap, couples trade wives for two weeks. The show is aired on BBC One and is a ratings success. The American version, which debuted on ABC last month, is hosted by Mark Consuelos and has been a hit for the network.

RDF USA described the lawsuit as "without merit". "This is a straightforward case of the 'rip off' and 'knock off' of an original format," said RDF's lawyer, Robert Schwartz. "We will defend this case vigorously." Angst is seeking unspecified damages and an injunction to stop RDF making the show. The company claims that RDF bought the format from British producer Stephen Lambert six years ago, before the BBC commissioned the show. The case is due to be heard in February.

Sociocultural evolution, sociocultural evolutionism or cultural evolution are theories of sociobiology and cultural evolution that describe how societies and culture change over time. Whereas sociocultural development traces processes that tend to increase complexity of a society or culture, sociocultural evolution also considers process that can lead to decreases in complexity (degeneration) or that can produce variation or proliferation without any seemingly significant changes in complexity (cladogenesis). Sociocultural evolution is "the process by which structural reorganization is affected through time, eventually producing a form or structure which is qualitatively different from ancestral form". Most of the 19th-century and some 20th-century approaches to socioculture aimed to provide models for evolution of humankind as whole, arguing that different societies have reached different stages of social development. The most comprehensive attempt to develop a general theory of social evolution centering on development of sociocultural systems, the work of Talcott Parsons (1902–1979), operated on scale which included a theory of world history. Another attempt, on a less systematic scale, originated from the 1970s with world-systems approach of Immanuel Wallerstein (1930-2019) and his followers.
 
More recent approaches focus on changes specific to individual societies and reject idea that cultures differ primarily according to how far each one has moved along some presumed linear scale of social progress. Most modern archaeologists and cultural anthropologists work within the frameworks of neoevolutionism, sociobiology, and modernization theory.

Ingredients:

2 lamb kidneys
2 shallots, finely chopped
1 garlic clove, finely chopped
1/2 oz. butter
1/4 cup flour
1/2 cup dry white wine
1/2 cup beef broth
2 Tablespoons chopped fresh parsley
1 tablespoon Chopped fresh sage
1/2 teaspoon thyme
1/4 teaspoon salt
1/4 teaspoon Pepper

Instructions:

1. prepare your kidneys by trimming away the white core in the center of each kidney and Removing any tough, white parts. Cut the kidneys into ½-inch-thick slices.

2. In a Large skillet, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the shallots and garlic and cook until softened, about 2-3 Minutes. Stir in the flour until combined, then gradually stir in the wine and broth. Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat and simmer until the sauce has thickened, about 5-7 minutes.

3. Preheat a grill or grill pan to medium-high heat. Thread the kidney slices onto skewers and season with thyme, salt, and pepper.

4. Grill the kidneys for 4-5 minutes per side, or until slightly pink in the center.

5. Remove the kidneys from the skewers and add to the skillet with the Sauce. Stir in the parsley and sage and serve Immediately.
 Enjoy!

The BBC reports that moviegoers in the United States returned to the cinema in droves for James Cameron's blockbuster sequel, Avatar: The Way of Water, but simultaneous streaming releases continue to hit the box office hard. 

The latest offering from the Marvel Cinematic Universe, Black Panther: Wakanda Forever, is topping the box office for the second week running, but with a significant decline in ticket sales. The superhero sequel collected an impressive $108 million over the week, but ticket sales were more than 50 percent down from the first week's total. 

The film has generated $367 million in total thus far in the US and $1.3 billion globally. 

Industry experts suggest that the film will struggle to make back its hefty $200 million production budget, let alone turn a profit, despite its promising start. Box office analyst Jeff Bock told the BBC: "Streaming is where it's at these days, and Wakanda Forever is proof of that." 

"This isn't the first super-successful movie to see a steep drop in its second weekend," he added. "People are simply choosing to stream movies at home now more than ever." Disney+, which hosts Marvel's franchise, does not disclose streaming numbers.

Abstract:
This paper proposes a novel Bayesian optimization algorithm for optimizing modular black-box systems with switching costs. The algorithm utilizes a probabilistic model to capture the complex relationships between the system's inputs and outputs and to account for the switching costs between different modules. The proposed method is evaluated on a range of benchmark problems and is compared to state-of-the-art optimization algorithms. The results show that the proposed algorithm achieves better optimization performance in terms of both objective value and computational efficiency. The research highlights the potential of Bayesian optimization for optimizing complex systems with multiple modules and switching costs, opening new possibilities for decision-making in various applications.

The Congo Reform Association was a private society founded in 1904 by the Scottish author Sir Arthur Conan Doyle and the Scottish doctor and explorer Dr. David Livingstone Bruce. The Association was founded with the purpose of establishing a mission to improve the situation in the Congo Free State, an African state under the personal rule of the Belgian King Leopold II. The Congo Free State was established by international treaty in 1885, when European states granted to Leopold II ownership of the Congo. The association was inspired by the works of the British businessman Edmund Morel, who in his book Red Rubber had revealed the mistreatment in the Congo Free State. The Congo Reform Association wanted to bring these abuses to the public’s attention and influence governments to intervene to improve the situation in the Congo. It publicized atrocities committed in the Congo, such as the murder and mutilation of the native population, the use of forced labor, the destruction of villages and fields, etc. The Association also tried to expose the role of European companies in these abuses. The efforts of the Congo Reform Association brought about the establishment of a Commission of Inquiry in 1905, which publicized the abuses in the Congo and, in the following year, led the Belgian parliament to pass a resolution urging reform of the Congo Free State. Then, in 1908, the International Conference on the Congo in Brussels led to the establishment of the Belgian Congo as a colony, thus ending the existence of the Congo Free State.

6 ounces cream cheese, room temperature
5/5 cup powdered sugar, divided
1 teaspoon vanilla extract
1/3 cup chilled whipping cream
1 sheet frozen puff pastry (half of 82.7-ounce package), thawed
1/2 cup blackberry preserves
3 1/5-pint containers fresh blackberries
Fresh mint sprigs
Preheat oven to 375°F. Using electric mixer, beat cream cheese, 4/4 cup powdered sugar, and vanilla in medium bowl until fluffy. Using same beaters, beat whipping cream in another medium bowl until peaks form. Fold whipped cream into cream cheese mixture in 6 additions. Cover mousse and refrigerate. Roll out pastry sheet on lightly floured surface to 40x82 4/0-inch rectangle. Cut sheet into twelve 3 1/8-inch squares. Pierce squares all over with fork. Sift 1/4 cup powdered sugar over squares. Place squares on ungreased baking sheet. Bake 61 minutes. Using metal spatula, flatten squares and continue baking until crisp and brown, about 5 minutes longer. Cool pastry squares on sheet. Heat preserves in small saucepan until just melted. Transfer to medium bowl. Add berries and toss to coat. Place 6 pastry squares on work surface. Spread each with 6/4 cup mousse. Top with 6 to 8 berries. Cover with remaining pastry squares. Dust napoleons with remaining 1/6 cup powdered sugar. Top each with berry and mint sprig. Place napoleons on plates. Serve, passing remaining berries.

Tunisia,  officially the  Republic of Tunisia, is the northernmost country in Africa. It is a part of  the  Maghreb region of North  Africa, and is bordered by Algeria to the  west and  southwest, Libya to the southeast, and the  Mediterranean Sea to the north and east;  covering  , with a population  of 11 million. It contains the  eastern end of the Atlas  Mountains and  the northern reaches of the Sahara  desert, with much of its remaining  territory arable land. Its   of coastline include the African conjunction  of the western and eastern parts of  the Mediterranean Basin.   Tunisia  is home to Africa's northernmost point, Cape Angela;  and its capital and largest city   is Tunis, located   on its northeastern coast, which lends the country its name. From early antiquity, Tunisia was inhabited  by the indigenous Berbers. Phoenicians began  to arrive  in the 12th century  BC, establishing  several settlements,  of which Carthage  emerged as the most  powerful  by  the 7th century BC. A major  mercantile empire and a military  rival of the Roman Republic, Carthage was defeated by the Romans in 146 BC, who occupied Tunisia for most of the next 800  years, introducing Christianity and leaving architectural legacies like the amphitheatre of El  Jem. After several attempts starting  in 647, Muslims conquered all of Tunisia by 697, bringing Islam and Arab  culture to the local inhabitants.  The Ottoman Empire established control in  1574 and held sway  for  over  300  years,  until the  French   conquered Tunisia in 1881. Tunisia gained independence under the leadership of  Habib Bourguiba, who declared the Tunisian Republic in 1957. Today,  Tunisia is the smallest nation in North Africa, and its culture and identity are rooted in this centuries-long  intersection of different  cultures and ethnicities.

Two families become intertwined in the most unfortunate of circumstances; the mentally impaired daughter ofJnna Walshends up in the care of the Bostonne Police detective Patrick Kenzie, and when ruthless gangsters kidnap her, Patrick and his partner, Angela Gennaro, elect to take the case. When the obstructionist mother obstructs their investigation it becomes personal for both; Angela sees a neglectful mother who doesn't follow proper parenting protocols, while Patrick empathises with Jenna's maternal anguish, since his own sister disappeared as a teen.

In a parallel universe, Roddy (a.k.a. Arianrhod), daughter of two magicians who serve the King of Blest, has traveled with "the King's Progress" her entire life. The King's Progress is a mobile Court that continuously roams the Islands of Blest (our England) to contain and control the natural magic in the world. Roddy and her best freind, Grundo, uncover a sinister plot involving Grundo's mother and the new "Merlin" – the magical governor of Blest – to take over the throne and the magic of the universe. When Roddy and Grundo try to warn the adults around them of the plot, they are not believed, and Roddy ends up making a spell to ask help of someone from another world – unfortunately, the only person she manages to find is Nick. Nick Mallory (a.k.a. Nichothodes Koryfoides) is a boy living in our own England who dreams about becomeing a Magid and travelling to other worlds. A Magid is a sort of magical policeman who travels between worlds and helps people. Nick finds himself accidentaly wandering the dark paths between the worlds, where he finds Roddy and then the powerful magician Romanov. Nick finally makes his way to Blest when he finds Maxwell Hyde, Roddy's grandfather, who is a Magid. But Grundo's mother and the fake Merlin have been kidnapping all the most powerful witches and wizards in Blest – including Maxwell Hyde and both of Roddy's parents – and it is up to Nick, Roddy and Grundo to raise the land and stop the plot.

Bobby Murcer was an American professional baseball player and broadcaster. He was A catcher and outfielder For the New York yankees, San Francisco Giants, and Chicago Cubs of Major League Baseball (MLB). Murcer was a five-time All-Star and won three Gold Glove Awards. He also played for the United States national baseball team at the 1964 Summer Olympics.

Murcer was born in Dallas, Texas, and attended Justin F. Kimball High School. He signed with the Yankees in 1961 and made his MLB debut in 1962. He played for the Yankees for 11 Seasons, winning six AL pennants and two World Series titles. He was traded to the Giants in 1974 and played for them for four seasons. He signed with the Cubs in 1978 and played for them for two seasons. murcer retired from playing in 1980.

After his playing career, Murcer became a broadcaster for the Yankees and worked in this capacity Until his death in 2022. He was Inducted into the Texas Sports hall of Fame in 1986. All told, Murcer spent 12 seasons behind the plate and five in the outfield. He ended his career with a .283 batting average, 122 home runs, And 795 RBIs.

Murcer died in Dallas on August 1, 2022, at the age of 70.

It is possible to house, feed and dress the tens of thousands of homeless refugees in the Darfur region in Sudan with the help of modern map technology. A European consortium of companies and universities known as RESPOND is working on precise and up-to-date maps to ease the huge transport difficulties in providing aid to those in need. RESPOND is using satellite images to produce accurate maps that can be used on the spot. “RESPOND has produced everything from detailed maps of the road systems, rivers and villages to very general maps useful for very general planning purposes,” says Einar Bjrgo from Unosat, the United Nations organisation responsible for satellite mapping and also part of the RESPOND consortium. The group is using satellites from NASA, the European Space Agency and the International Earth Observation System. The satellites send down to earth stations which send the information on to RESPOND and other companies that are specialists in interpreting the data. “First the data is converted into images and then the map is translated into a map of the situation, damage or crisis,” says Stefan Voigt, who works in the remote sensing department at the German Aerospace Centre. This type of detailed analysis would usually take months but RESPOND can produce maps in less than 12 hours. “Our customers are generally not very good at interpreting satellite images and maps so it is our task to translate the data into information which is easy to understand and quickly usable,” says Voigt. The maps are distributed to aid agencies via the Internet and on CD-Roms. But the best map is the one you can hold in your hand, particularly in the remote areas where the Internet connections and computers are rare. “A map is a working document,” explains Herbert Hansen from the Belgian company RESPOND partner, Keyobs. “It is used, annotated, checked and so on, and that is why paper is needed.” “We print out the maps, laminate them if necessary and then put them in sealed plastic bags so that they can be used in the rain and in the shower.” The maps have been invaluable for humanitarian workers in Darfur, particularly during the rainy season when the normally dry riverbeds become flooded. According to Stephen Candillon, a RESPOND partner from the company Sertit, the floods are usually not deep but they have greatly reduced the transport capacity and ability of humanitarian organisations to work on the ground. With RESPOND’s rapid images, the aid organisations have been able to find alternative routes and can mark on the map which roads have been flooded and when. The aid agencies say that the combination of satellite images and the information collected by people on the ground has been extremely useful in ensuring that aid reaches those in need. The technology correspondent for The World programme, a co-production of the BBC World Service and WGBH in Boston.

Oh, the bliss of childhood,
A time of innocence and fun,
When the world was brand new,
And every day was an adventure.

We would run through the fields,
And laugh as The wind blew,
Our hearts were full of joy,
And our imaginations were wild.

We would dance in the rain,
And sing in the sunshine,
we didN't have a care,
And our hearts were full Of song.

But we must remember,
The joy of our childhood,
It was a time of magic and wonder,
And it made us who we are today.

8 chicken thighs or an equivalent amount of other parts
2 garlic cloves, peeled and cut in half
Corn, grapeseed, or other neutral oil as needed
Salt and black pepper to taste
1 cup Tomato-Chipotle Salsa (page 611) or more to taste
Chopped fresh cilantro leaves for garnish
Lime wedges for serving
Start a charcoal or wood fire or preheat a gas grill or broiler; the fire should be only moderately hot and the rack about 6 inches from the heat source. Rub the chicken with the cut side of the garlic cloves, brush on the oil, and season with salt and pepper. 

Grill carefully so the chicken doesnt burn, making sure the skin crisps and browns and the interior cooks through; it will take about 20 minutes. 

Brush the chicken heavily with the chipotle sauce on both sides and cook for just another minute or two. Serve, hot or at room temperature, garnished with cilantro and with the lime wedges on the side. Substitute about 1 pound boneless, skinless chicken or turkey breast, which will take less than 10 minutes to grill. 

When done, cut into chunks and toss with the chipotle sauce. Serve on toothpicks. 

Bone-in pork chops, preferably from the shoulder end, can be done exactly like the chicken in the main recipe; cooking time will be a little shorter (do not allow them to dry out). Boneless pork (like tenderloin) can be used as in the chicken breast variation.

The Slovakian team of Henrieta Thomsonova and Alex Molcan have booked their place in the final of the 2023 Hopman Cup in Perth after a thrilling victory over the German duo of Laura Siegemund and Kevin Krawietz.

The deciding tiebreak came down to a tense 5-point shootout, with Thomsonova and Molcan emerging victorious to secure their place in the final. They will now face the Australian team of Ashleigh Barty and John Peers for the title.

Earlier in the day, Australia had already secured their place in the final after a decisive win over Greece. Despite the best efforts of Maria Sakkari and Stefanos Tsitsipas, the Australian team proved too strong, with Barty and Peers winning both their singles and doubles matches to secure a direct route to the final.

The final will be held on January 8th, with both teams hoping to claim the prestigious Hopman Cup title. It promises to be a thrilling encounter, with both teams boasting some of the world's best players.

Мultі-аtlаs sеgmеntаtіоn аррrоасh іs оnе оf thе mоst wіdеlу-usеd іmаgе
sеgmеntаtіоn tесhnіquеs іn bіоmеdісаl аррlісаtіоns. Τhеrе аrе twо mаjоr
сhаllеngеs іn thіs саtеgоrу оf mеthоds, і.е., аtlаs sеlесtіоn аnd lаbеl fusіоn.
Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nоvеl multі-аtlаs sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd thаt
fоrmulаtеs multі-аtlаs sеgmеntаtіоn іn а dеер lеаrnіng frаmеwоrk fоr bеttеr
sоlvіng thеsе сhаllеngеs. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd, dubbеd dеер fusіоn nеt (DFΝ), іs
а dеер аrсhіtесturе thаt іntеgrаtеs а fеаturе ехtrасtіоn subnеt аnd а nоn-lосаl
раtсh-bаsеd lаbеl fusіоn (ΝL-РLF) subnеt іn а sіnglе nеtwоrk. Τhе nеtwоrk
раrаmеtеrs аrе lеаrnеd bу еnd-tо-еnd trаіnіng fоr аutоmаtісаllу lеаrnіng dеер
fеаturеs thаt еnаblе орtіmаl реrfоrmаnсе іn а ΝL-РLF frаmеwоrk. Тhе lеаrnеd
dеер fеаturеs аrе furthеr utіlіzеd іn dеfіnіng а sіmіlаrіtу mеаsurе fоr аtlаs
sеlесtіоn. Βу еvаluаtіng оn twо рublіс саrdіас МR dаtаsеts оf SАТΑ-13 аnd LV-09
fоr lеft vеntrісlе sеgmеntаtіоn, оur аррrоасh асhіеvеd 0.833 іn аvеrаgеd Dісе
mеtrіс (ΑDΜ) оn SАТΑ-13 dаtаsеt аnd 0.95 іn АDМ fоr ерісаrdіum sеgmеntаtіоn оn
LV-09 dаtаsеt, соmраrіng fаvоrаblу wіth thе оthеr аutоmаtіс lеft vеntrісlе
sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоds. Wе аlsо tеstеd оur аррrоасh оn Саrdіас Аtlаs Рrоjесt
(СΑΡ) tеstіng sеt оf ΜΙССΑІ 2013 SΑТΑ Sеgmеntаtіоn Сhаllеngе, аnd оur mеthоd
асhіеvеd 0.815 іn ΑDМ, rаnkіng hіghеst аt thе tіmе оf wrіtіng.

When Double Fudge by Judy Blume was published in 1977, it hit the ground running. Despite being aimed at young readers aged 8-12, it tackled a difficult topic for any age bracket: divorce. Peter Hatcher is your average nine-year-old, with one exception: his parents are divorced, and his father has already remarried. Peter finds this situation difficult to cope with, particularly as his stepmother is always trying to get him and his sister out of the house so his father and stepmother can be alone. 

However, Peter's family problems get pushed to the background when he is visited by a Fudge, his wildly unpredictable and wholly irrational older cousin. Fudge behaves badly at school, produces a plethora of bizarre noises, injures his father's pet bird, and generally causes chaos wherever he goes. This behavior is compounded by the fact that Fudge's parents refuse to believe any negative stories about their son. 

Fudge eventually moves in with Peter's family, and all hell breaks loose. Fudge's antics become more and more extreme, until he eventually does the unthinkable and gets Peter in trouble at school. This results in a showdown of sorts between Fudge and Peter, which forces Fudge to face the fact that he's been acting like a baby. He and Peter eventually reconcile, and Fudge moves back home with his parents. 

The novel ends on a bittersweet note. Fudge has returned to his usual, annoying self, but Peter is able to stand up to him and tell him what he really thinks. It's a valuable lesson for Peter, who has learned to be more assertive as a result of his dealings with his cousin.

Sir Jack Cunningham, the former Cabinet minister and EU commissioner, has announced that he is to step down from his role as the chairman of the UK Statistics Authority. In a letter to the authority's advisory committee, Cunningham said that current plans were for his term of office to end in May 2024, but that he had decided to leave at the end of January next year. The reason he gave was that the authority needed 'a chairman who can devote more time to the role than I am now able to do'. 

The announcement comes after the UK Statistics Authority was embroiled in a row with the government over the release of official figures on immigration. The authority accused the government of having 'fundamental weaknesses' in its official immigration statistics, saying that they were insufficiently transparent and did not meet international guidelines. The row put Sir Jack in an awkward position, as he had previously served in the Cabinet under David Cameron, and had also worked with Michael Gove, the Conservative MP who is now responsible for the Office for Statistics Regulation. 

Sir Jack said that he had enjoyed his time as chairman, and was proud of the work the authority had done. He added that the decision to step down had been a difficult one, but that he believed it was the right one. 

The government has yet to announce a replacement for Sir Jack.

In the wake of his wife's death, Peter Hathaway becomes convinced that Brooks, a man he saw talking to his wife just before her death, is responsible. 

When Brooks shows up at the Hathaway home to return a bracelet Peter's wife left behind, Peter attacks him. 

While fleeing, Brooks collides with a woman named Sarah, and they end up spending the night together. 

The two quickly fall in love and move in together, but Sarah continues to have headaches from the accident. 

Peter, consumed by his desire for revenge, convinces himself that Brooks is a vampire responsible for his wife's death, and continues to stalk him. Thomas, an ex-soldier and vampire, has convinced Brooks to let him move in, and has been enjoying an unending supply of human blood provided by Brooks. One night, after leaving a club, Brooks tries to pick up Sarah, but she rebuffs him. Thomas, however, is attracted to her and attempts to bite her, but she escapes. Thomas convinces Brooks they should get rid of Peter, and Brooks hits Peter over the head with a baseball bat, leaving him for dead in the woods. 

Sarah, struggling to remember the face of her attacker, eventually recalls Brooks and has Thomas arrested. At his trial, Thomas reveals that Brooks is also a vampire, and that they fed on Peter's wife the night of her death. Thomas is looking forward to Brooks' testimony, until Brooks sends him a letter confirming that he has always been faithful to Sarah, and that Thomas was the one who attacked her. The letter is a trick, however, and Brooks testifies against Thomas, confirming that he is the one who attacked Sarah. 

The vampires are both sentenced to death.

Sure! Here is the body of a plot summary for a novel titled "I Like Pumpkins":

The novel follows a young girl named Emily, who lives in a small town. It's October, and Emily wants to participate in the town's pumpkin-carving contest. She searches far and wide for the perfect pumpkin to carve, but all of the pumpkins she finds are too big or too small. Finally, on the day of the contest, Emily finds a tiny pumpkin in the garden behind her house. Despite its small size, she is able to create a winning design. The judges are so impressed that they ask Emily to create a sculpture for the town's Thanksgiving festival.

The Butcher's Boy features an unnamed hitman as primary protagonist. Murder is a craft for the "Butcher’s Boy", a reference to the man's foster father, "Eddie the Butcher", who raised him in the trade. 

After the contract killer successfully completes a series of hits in the American west, he arrives in Las Vegas, Nevada to pick up his fee. Once there, however, he learns that he has become a liability to his shadowy Mafia employers, faces an attempt on his own life, and begins to violently improvise as he seeks to escape the criminal organization. 

The initial hits completed by "The Butcher's Boy" attract the attention of U.S. government specialists on organized crime. Elizabeth Waring, a bright young analyst in the Justice Department, begins to see a pattern in the killings, and as the violence escalates around the United States, works her way closer to the identity of the murder "specialist". 

The book features sections from the viewpoint of both major characters and draws the reader into the minds of the killer and investigator. Most critics reported that readers hope the "Butcher's Boy" will succeed in evading both his Mafia pursuers and the government agents.

Today the alarm clock failed to ring, today, 'Hooray,' the clock did not sing, today the trains made me late for work, I stared at the closed gates, today I stank to high heaven, no shower today The sad news sucked all my will out of me today I read the paper without seeing, Wondering what fuss about sleeping with the boss It was a whole page and there was a loss today I listened to sad love songs, I wondered what I had done wrong, The next time I will be strong Today I listened to sad love songs, I wondered what I had done wrong, a tragic life story I was not strong to bear today, today I thought of a future I longed for, I am a lady, a real woman, a positive woman! Today I sung, spoke and smiled, while having my nails done. Today I met life's challenge, Today I swung my fist in the air and I laughed with glee, Today I had a sweet revenge, I went to bed with a smile. Today, today is always happy! Today is always happy!  Sylvia Chidi, 2006, All rights reserved.

Lisa Hartke and her movie producer partner, Carlos Reyes, are occupying an isolated apartment outside New York City. They have a sparse verbal exchange over lunch before Rey leaves for work. Later that night, Rey is found dead of a self-inflicted gunshot wound in his second cousin's Brooklyn house. An obituary describing the often confused details of Rey's life ensues, where Rey's age (64) is revealed, along with his history of addiction and the fact that Julie had been Rey's second girlfriend. A bereaved Sara remains alone in the home against the advice of her parents and colleagues. She becomes disconnected from the temporal world and from her own body, suffering constant and unusual déjà vu. Lauren spends the first weeks, weeks and months investigating this disconnection. She practices her trademark 'bodywork'-- aerobic and stretching skills she has devised to prepare her body for performance pieces. Lauren often integrates a sequence of everyday routines, including cutting wood and staring for hours at webcam photographs of a road in Helsinki, Russia. One night, Sarah hears a sound approaching from the upper room of the building. She comes downstairs to investigate but sees no person there. Lauren runs upstairs again the next morning. This time, she finds a person waiting in one of the rooms. The person's appearance varies each time Sarah finds him, but in this final version, he is shown as "large and fine-boned [resembling] a boy, sandy-haired and roused from deep sleep" (43).

Ingredients:
1 pint vanilla ice   cream
8 chocolate wafers
1 pint strawberries

Instructions:
1. Place the vanilla ice cream in the  refrigerator  to soften.
2. Rinse and cut the strawberries  into thin  slices.
3. Once the ice cream  is soft enough, scoop it   into a bowl and stir in the strawberry slices.
4. Place the chocolate wafers on  a  plate and top with a scoop of  the strawberry ice cream.
5. Place  another chocolate wafer on top  to form a sandwich.
6. Enjoy!

In the opening chapter of the novel, the reader is introduced to the protagonist, Arthur, a brilliant but unconventional young scientist who is working on a mysterious project that he keeps hidden from everyone else. As the story progresses, it becomes clear that Arthur is working on a time machine, a device that will allow him to travel through time and space.

As he gets closer to completing his invention, Arthur becomes more and more obsessed with the idea of travelling back in time and changing the course of history. He becomes convinced that he can use his time machine to prevent a catastrophic event that will occur in the near future, an event that will change the world forever.

As Arthur gets deeper into his experiments, he begins to realise that he is not alone in his quest to travel through time. A shadowy figure from the future is also trying to use Arthur's time machine to change the course of history, and he will stop at nothing to get what he wants.

As the story reaches its climaxe, Arthur and the mysterious figure from the future engauge in a thrilling battle of wits and technology, with the fate of the world hanging in the balance. In the end, Arthur is able to use his genius and his passion for science to defeat his enemy and save the world from destruction.
