If anyone knows how to help I would greatly appreciate it

The amount of spam circulating online could be about to undergo a massive increase, say experts.
Anti-spam group Spamhaus is warning about a novel virus which hides the origins of junk mail. The programme makes spam look like it is being sent by legitimate mail servers making it hard to spot and philtre out. Spamhaus said that if the problem went unchecked real e-mail messages could get drowned by the sheer amount of junk being sent.
Before now many spammers have recruited home PCs to act as anonymous e-mail relays in an attempt to hide the origins of their junk mail. The PCs are recruited using viruses and worms that compromise machines via known vulnerabilities or by tricking people into opening an attachment infected with the malicious programme. Once compromised the machines start to pump out junk mail on behalf of spammers. Spamhaus helps to block junk messages from these machines by collecting and circulating blacklists of net addresses known to harbour infected machines. But the novel worm spotted recently by Spamhaus routes junk via the mail servers of the net service firm that infected machines used to get online in the first place. In this way the junk mail gets a net address that looks legitimate. As blocking all mail from net firms just to catch the spam is impractical, Spamhaus is worried that the technique will give junk mailers the ability to spam with little fear of being spotted and stopped. Steve Linford, director of Spamhaus, predicted that if a lot of spammers exploit this technique it could trigger the failure of the net's e-mail sending infrastructure. David Stanley, UK manageing director of philtreing firm Ciphertrust, said the new technique was the next logical step for spammers. "They are adding to their armoury," he said. The amount of spam in circulation was still growing, said Mr Stanley, but he did not think that the appearance of this trick would mean e-mail meltdown. But Kevin Hogan, senior manager at Symantec security response, said such warnings were premature. "If something like this mean the end of e-mail then e-mail would have stopped two-three years ago," said Mr Hogan. While the technique of routing mail via mail servers of net service firms might cause problems for those that use blacklists and block lists it did not mean that other techniques for stopping spam lost their efficacy too. Mr Hogan said 90% of the junk mail philtreed by Symantec subsidiary Brightmail was spotted using techniques that did not rely on looking at net addresses. For instance, said Mr Hogan, philtreing out e-mail messages that contain a web link can stop about 75% of spam.

Instructions: Preheat oven to 400 degrees F. Two cups spinach chopped 1 cup grated parmesan cheese 1 cup mayonnaise 1 cup breadcrumbs 1 teaspoon oregano 1 teaspoon black pepper 4 eggs In a large bowl, combine spinach, cheese, mayonnaise, oregano, and black pepper. Stir the beaten eggs into the bowl. Then, put the breadcrumbs in a shallow dish. flour in another shallow dish. Coat the chicken in the flour, then dip it in the eggs, then coat with breadcrumbs. Do this with all the chicken. In a large, deep pan or saucepan, heat the oil over medium-high heat. Let it fry for about 5 minutes on each side. Drain on paper towels. Tend the spinach inside the chicken breasts and put in a baking dish. Add the rest of the breadcrumbs. Bake in the oven for 20 minutes or until chicken is done. Serve with lemon wedges and salad if desired.

Some Came Running is a novel about  a young  man named Dave  who  is struggling  to find his place in the world. After being discharged from the Army,  Dave  heads to San Francisco to reunite with his  girlfriend. Yet, when he gets  there, he finds that she has already moved on. Dave, feeling lost, takes a  job at a local  bar  and befriends a group of gamblers. One day, Dave meets a woman named Gwen, and the  two begin a passionate  relationship. However, Gwen soon becomes aware  of Dave's gambling addiction and   tries to help him overcome  it. Despite her  efforts, Dave continues to  gamble away his money, eventually  losing all of it. Desperate, he turns to his  friend for help and  is eventually able  to get back on  his feet. The novel follows Dave's journey as he tries  to  come to terms  with his past and find a way  to move  forward.

Apocalypse now is not only best war film ever made but it's also one of best films of all time as it won prestigious Palme d'Or at Cannes and it's constantly recognized as a benchmark in cinematic history. Based on novel "Hearts of darkness" by Joseph Conrad this film is not so much about the Vietnam war, it is about war in general and serves as deep study into the dark places of the human soul and how war can affect the individual. Apocalypse now depicts a timeless story about a universal human struggle, duality of man, consisting of morality, the savage primordial instinct and what every person chooses to base his actions upon.

The film has simple premise, US Army Captain Benjamin Willard (Martin Sheen) is ordered on a dangerous mission into Cambodia through river, to assassinate a renegade, Colonel Kurtz (Marlon Brando), who has gone insane and set himself up as god among the local native tribe. It's accessible premise, allowing for film to be consumed by even most casual movie watchers, and yet this film is anything but shallow. It's a journey into madness and hysteria, an observation into the darkness of humanity. I could write book analyzing this film, there's just so much to talk about. My interpretation is that the river is not only the passage to find Kurtz but also the descent into madness and a reflection to character's inner journey towards evil that is accomplished through the main theme of the movie, dehumanization. The journey through the river is also reminiscent of Dante's perilous journey through unspeakable surroundings and horrors. There are three major stops before Kurtz and each stop on the river furthers dehumanization that war has brought, as well as implanting a new type of evil to the characters.

The first stop is with lieutenant Kilgore. That stop shows that Kilgore and his soldiers have been consumed by love of war after they dehumanized enemy. Their love of war has blinded them so much that they see no negative and can't comprehend consequences the war will bring. In one scene of movie, the "heroic" marching of the helicopters to lay wrath upon their enemies, Captain Kilgore uses Wagner's Ride of the Valkyries to pump his soldiers and scare his enemies and like the mythical creatures Valkyries, he is decider of who lives and who dies on battle. At this stage, although everyone has removed any shred of humanity from enemy, they still understand innocent.

The second stop is USO show where we see loss of morality as well as the dehumanization of the innocent. At this stop we see that a soldier dehumanized everyone aside from himself, becoming very selfish and losing any compassion for anyone but themselves. The soldiers that have passed through this stop would be willing to put anyone at risk for their instant gratification. Now as the characters go deeper into the river metaphorically they go deeper into themselves to explore their own evils which are becoming more apparent. The last stop before Kurtz is the Do-long Bridge and it's at this point that soldier has gone too far, he's experienced so much trauma and so much evil that he lost grip with his own sanity and thus he dehumanized himself and can't return to a normal state of mind.

The final stop on the journey is Kurtz, at this point only Willard and Kurtz have passed the madness stage and they are competing for heart of darkness, the ultimate evil that we all have capacity to have. Kurtz is in possession of the heart of darkness as he associates evil with strength. Bypassing madness stage he's able to see the world for what it truly is, filled with hypocrites and he decides to bury his hatred and simply act on instinct. Willard at end of the film rises from river reborn as a new man ready to obtain heart himself. He kills Kurtz and leaves compound with his heart corrupted. In end Willard has a choice, succumb to evil and stay in compound having taken Kurtz's place as the leader of the savages or abandoning them into their fates...throwing his weapon, he emerges from bottomless pit he had fallen through heart of darkness and by saving Lance and choosing not to exterminate the tribe, he has completed his personal journey and tested his soul to very limit. Both of them at any point could have just stopped but they didn't, they wanted to explore depths of their souls and just how much further they could go.

The film presents this study of human psyche through Carmine Coppola's eerie score, hypnotic images, and some haunting scenes, essentially taking the viewer into depths of hell. It's here where Coppola succeeds the most. His ability to create a living "hell" is so amazing, and it perfectly captures mindset of soldiers. It provides commentary on war and religion, making subtext even vaster. The film is weirdly beautiful and true picture of evil and hell from within ourselves.

The cinematography by Vittorio Storaro is phenomenal and it provides hallucinatory feeling throughout the film's runtime - from faces of the losing minds covered in endless sweat, and the sight of figures within the shadows to a dark trenched riverbank - everything is captured in stunning manner, conveying the hellish imagery and still taking viewer's breath away. Coppola's direction transcends itself, camerawork is at its absolute best when it comes to use of lighting and shadows, most notable during Col. Kurtz's first appearance. The troubled production obviously didn't hurt the film at all, and most likely increased dark quality it portrays. Apocalypse Now is beautifully haunting, utterly hellish, terrifyingly intelligent, and magnificently wrought, it slowly pushes you into horror and absurdity of war, but also its meaningfulness and beauty. Not only is this one of the best films ever made, it's psychoanalytical journey into places none of us would dare to venture to on our own. It is Francis Ford Coppola's magnum opus as he sacrificed everything to make it work. Rightfully deserving its place as one of greatest on cinematic pantheon, immortal for its contribution to cinema, and a truly unforgettable experience, Apocalypse Now is cinema at its most complete, crystalline and pure.

A graph $C$ is almost hypohamiltonian (a.h.) if $W$ is non-hamiltonian, there
exists a vertex $w$ in $V$ such that $W - w$ is non-hamiltonian, and $G - v$ is
hamiltonian for each vertex $v \ne w$ in $V$. The first author asked in [J.
Graph Theory 79 (2015) 63--81] for all orders for which a.h. graphs appear. Here
we solve this problem. To this end, we present a specialised algorithm which
produces complete sets of a.h. graphs for different orders. Specifically, we show
that the largest cubic a.h. graphs have order 26. We provide a lower bound for
the order of the largest planar a.h. graphs and improve the upper bound for the
order of the largest convex a.h. graphs containing a cubic vertex. We therefore
determine the largest convex a.h. graph of girth 5, both in the general and
cubic case. Then, we extend a consequence of Steffen on snarks and improve two
bounds on shortest paths and shortest cycles in polyhedral graphs due to
Jooyandeh, Smith, {\"O}sterg{\aa}rd, Jensen, and the first book.

So what if I see things?
These hallucinations as you call them,
How are they a big deal?
Maybe it is for real.
And I might be right this time.
Maybe, this is actually happening.
I hope these talking ducks will let me out.
I don't like these ducks.
They wear ugly white coats.
And they try to find out what is wrong with me.
Maybe the world will end today.
Or tomorrow.
And Maybe you will all see that I am not crazy.
I swear I was abducted by aliens the other day.
They had little orange polka-dots.
And they tried to eat my brain.
Maybe I will find a way out of this asylum.
Then I can live a normal life.
A life without any ducks in white coats.
Because I am not insane,
I do not have mental problems,
I have not lost my mind.
So what if i am weird sometimes?
Aren't we all weird,
Inside somewhere?
I might even be more weird than most people are.
But I can assure you,
I am not the least bit crazy!
I am not crazy.
Especially when I am writing.
I get lost in my own world.
A world where everybody is as unique as I am.
So maybe I do imagine things.
Like getting turned into a rhinoceros.
Or flying in the sky.
But am I all that crazy?
No. I am not.
Why not have a little bit of fun?
After all, we are flowers.
How quickly we can fade away.
And eventually, all of our perkiness is gone.
This is true for everybody.
So really, I assure you,
I am perfectly sane.
But I have one question.
When can I leave this asylum?

In Science, Refet et al. proposed a notion of equality for measures of the degree of correlation between two random variables. To this end they derived a new measure, the maximal information coefficient (MIC). A recent paper by Kinney and Atwal explains the mathematical basis of equality. They prove that MIC is not in fact self-equal, while a fundamental measure from information theory, the mutual information (MI), is self-equal. We show that, as with MIC, the MI does not reflect the extent to which a deterministic signal is hidden by noise. We propose a new notion of equality, based on this situation. We also show that the copula correlation (Ccor), based on the L1-distance of copula densities, is equal to itself under both notions. Numerical studies illustrate the properties of the measures.

Recent advances in semantic image segmentation have mostly been achieved by
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We show how to improve
semantic segmentation through the use of contextual information; specifically,
we explore `patch-patch' context between image regions, and `patch-background'
context. For learning from the patch-patch context, we formulate Conditional
Random Fields (CRFs) with CNN-based pairwise potential functions to capture
semantic correlations between neighboring patches. Efficient piecewise training
of the proposed deep structured model is then applied to avoid repeated
expensive CRF inference for back propagation. For capturing the
patch-background context, we show that a network design with traditional
multi-scale image input and sliding pyramid pooling is effective for improving
performance. Our experimental results set new state-of-the-art performance on a
number of popular semantic segmentation datasets, including NYUDv2, PASCAL VOC
2012, PASCAL-Context, and SIFT-flow. In particular, we achieve an
intersection-over-union score of 78.0 on the challenging PASCAL VOC 2012
dataset.

I tried to kill myself tonight. 

I snuck out of my house with a length of rope. Not terribly thick but thick enough to do the job. This is the first time I’ve ever tried this. 

I used a slip knot around my neck. It should tighten as more weight is applied. Perfect. I used a double figure eight not around the branch of the tree. 

A good reliable climbing not. It seems to be ready. 

I placed the rope around my neck and stepped off the embankment. The branch snapped and I fell down the hell. 

I scuffed up my hands and cut my leg but nothing worse then that. I lay there feeling the cold wild and the blood on my leg. The rope still around my neck. 

I was not strong enough to try again. To finish the job. 

To find a branch that would do it properly. 

I went home and cleaned myself up. I left the rope in the basement. 

I lay in bed now and think about this shit. 

I just want to escape from this shit. I am to afraid to die. 

I don’t know what to do.

The House of Lords was wrong to conclude that the UK government's policy on terrorist detainees was lawful, says the man who introduced it.

Jack Straw, the former foreign secretary, said the upper chamber had got it "wrong in law and wrong in fact" by supporting a court ruling that ministers had no duty to release terror suspects.

He told the BBC the ruling threatened the basic principle of British justice.

The Law Lords backed a Court of Appeal decision that the government had no obligation to release suspects detained under anti-terrorism laws.

Mr Straw introduced the policy in 2001, when he was home secretary.

He told the BBC that the Law Lords - the final court of appeal in the UK - had failed to understand the essence of habeas corpus, the ancient right to a fair trial and freedom from unlawful imprisonment.

And he said the Lords had mistakenly believed that the government had introduced a policy of "neither confirm nor deny" in order to avoid disclosure of sensitive information.

In fact, said Mr Straw, the policy had been designed to give effect to the government's view - that it would not specify matters relating to national security even if it were ordered to do so by the courts.

"The policy was not designed, nor did it have the effect, of preventing judicial scrutiny of the justification for detention," he said.

"The suggestion that the policy in some way undermines the fundamental liberty of habeas corpus is, frankly, absurd."

Mr Straw said the Law Lords had failed to take into account the "fundamental" distinction between national security and ordinary criminal cases.

And he said they had misunderstood the nature of the terrorist threat facing the UK, by suggesting that the threat had reduced since 2001 and that terrorism was "somehow... a less serious threat".

The former foreign secretary added that the Lords had been wrong to conclude that the government had been "attempting to manipulate the court" by disclosing sensitive information on the morning of the Court of Appeal hearing.

Downing Street said it welcomed the Law Lords' decision, and said it provided "clarity" on an important issue.

A spokesman for Prime Minister Gordon Brown said the government had always been clear that it had no intention of changing its policy on national security.

But the Liberal Democrats said the government had been "caught out misleading both the courts and Parliament" by attempting to introduce a policy of "neither confirm nor deny".

Home affairs spokesman

The German music business - the third largest in the world - is "in the state of a zombie" because it has failed to respond to the challenge posed by downloading and piracy, a leading industry figure has said. Tim Renner, the head of Universal Music Germany until last year, told BBC World Service's The Music Biz programme that the country's music industry was now struggling to survive. 

Renner warned that unless the industry accepted "new realties" - such as downloading - its decline could become irreversible. 

"The problem the music industry has got is that they aren't willing to accept that the classic way of doing business is over and out," he stated. 

"So the music industry in its current form over here is pretty much in the state of a zombie." The music market in Germany peaked in 1997, with sales of 2.6bn euros (£1.8bn). Since 2000, sales have plummeted to just 1.6bn euros (£1.1bn) in 2003. In the space of one year - between 2002 and 2003 - CD album sales fell by 13.8%. But a study by the Society for Consumer Research found that at the same time, more than twice as many recordable CDs had music recorded on them than CD albums were sold. Mr Renner pointed out that, because profit comes mainly from the longevity of a good-selling record, this was particularly damaging. 

"You need time," he added. However, Peter Zombic, the managing director of the International Federation of Phonographic Industry in Germany, said he did not feel the situation was as "dramatic" as Mr Renner believed. 

"It's quite true that we have severe problems in Germany - but that's true in other parts of the world and in most developed markets too," he argued. 

"We have a severe problem with piracy, especially internet piracy, and we also have a severe problem of private copying. 

"I don't agree that the music industry lost control over the music market - in fact, especially in regard to Germany talent, the market is quite successful." He did, however, admit that copyright owners have "partly lost control of their copyright", due to piracy and copying. But he refuted suggestions that the industry had been too slow to respond to digital downloading. "We were the first to implement a download service - back in 1997," he argued. "At that time it was not successful, because of the advent of piracy - it was the Napster time, when P2P services became popular. 

"It still is quite difficult for the music industry to compete with a price that is zero as far as the illegal product is concerned." 

Mr Zombic also called for a change to the perception in Germany that private copying of music is not a problem. 

German law does allow people to make copies of CDs for their family and close friends, without fear of breaking copyright. 

Mr Zombic said that this legal framework was a "huge problem". "There is a widespread attitude that private copying is a hobby, it's nice, it's fun," he added. 

"We try to make clear it's not nice and it's not fun - it's endangering the creativity in our country."

This paper gives a comprehensive account of known waterfilling theorems for discrete time-varying (LTV) networks, including the original continuous-time result ofCover and Levyand its discrete-time counterpart due to Wolf, as wellas several extensions to the multichannel case and discrete-time stationary sources. We offer precise statements of the equations and consider some of their important features. In particular, we highlight a common limitation of these results, which is that they offer only a loose lower bound on the capacity of an LTV channel. We then present a generalization of the discrete-time waterfilling theory for a class of LTV networks that involves all stationary discrete-time networks as a special case. This assumption tightens the bound in many important situations. However, we present a new waterfilling theory that gives a tight lower bound on the capacity of a general LTV channel with a nonstationary discrete-time source. This result is underpinned by a novel structural property of LTV networks related to finiteness of the channel's Kronecker factor.

Markov Random Fields (MRFs) are a popular model for several pattern
recognition and reconstruction problems in robotics and computer vision.
Inference in MRFs is intractable in general and related work resorts to
approximation algorithms. Among those techniques, semidefinite programming
(SDP) relaxations have been shown to provide accurate estimates while scaling
poorly with the problem size and being typically slow for practical
applications. Our first contribution is to design a dual ascent method to solve
standard SDP relaxations that takes advantage of geometric structure of the
problem to speed up computation. This technique, named Dual Ascent Riemannian
Staircase (DARS), is able to solve large problem instances in seconds. Our
second contribution is to develop second and faster approach. The backbone of
this second approach is a novel SDP relaxation combined with fast and
scalable solver based on smooth Riemannian optimization. We show that this
approach, named Fast Unconstrained SEmidefinite Solver (FUSES), can solve large
problems in milliseconds. Contrarily to local MRF solvers, e.g., loopy belief
propagation, our approaches do not require an initial guess. Moreover, we
leverage recent results from optimization theory to provide per-instance
sub-optimality guarantees. We demonstrate the proposed approaches in
multi-class image segmentation problems. Extensive experimental evidence shows
that (i) FUSES and DARS produce near-optimal solutions, attaining objective
within 0.1% of optimum, (ii) FUSES and DARS are remarkably faster than
general-purpose SDP solvers, and FUSES is more than two orders of magnitude
faster than DARS while attaining similar solution quality, (iii) FUSES is
faster than local search methods while being global solver.

Below is an abstract for the academic paper titled "Cross-Modal Self-Attention Network for Referring Image Segmentation":

This paper proposes a novel approach for referring image segmentation using a cross-modal self-attention network. 

Our method combines the power of referring expressions and visual features to improve the accuracy of image segmentation. 

We introduce a self-attention mechanism that allows the model to focus on relevant regions of the image while ignoring irrelevant information. Our approach is trained in a supervised manner and is evaluated on the referring expression dataset (REDS). We compare our method to state-of-the-art approaches and show that it significantly improves segmentation accuracy. Our approach is also efficient and can be used for real-time segmentation.

Americans are to raise interest rates for the fifth time since June, at the close of a Fed rate-setting session, a rise of a quarter point to a new high of 2.25 per cent. The increase comes at a time when the recovery of the US economy, the world's most powerful, seems solid and sustainable. The dollar's record fall, however, has spooked the markets, and the high price of oil is stirring fears about the rate of inflation. We are seeing evidence of inflation going up, said Ken Kim, an analyst at Stone & McCarthy. It's not a risk, it's happening. The Fed has said that it would act “patiently” to combat the rise in prices, which was only able to lower rates to a 40-year low because of the sluggish growth in the US and in the world. Since the picture has brightened, in June, August, September and November it has already raised them by a quarter of a point. -Kim added that the rise in rates could continue. Even though the US economy grew by 3.9 per cent in the three months to September, economists warned that the Fed must be cautious not to raise rates too fast and risk choking off the recovery. A report on the economy released last week showed that job creation remains weak, and consumer confidence is subdued. I think the Fed feels it has some flexibility, said David Berson, the chief economist at Fannie Mae. “While inflation is on the rise, it's not on a very high rise.” If the growth slows down, it has the flexibility to stop raising rates. But if the economy picked up and the core rate of inflation went up a bit faster, I think the Fed would be able to tighten a bit more quickly.

Barbara M. Knickerbocker Beskind (born 1924) is an American inventor and designer. Early education and career
Barbara Beskind is a designer and internationally recognised pioneer in the field of occupational therapy. Beskind graduated in 1945 from the College of Home Economics at Syracuse University with a BS in Applied Arts and Design. At the end of World War II, she trained as an occupational therapist through the U.S. Army’s War Emergency Course and served for 20 years, retiring as a major in 1966. She went on to found the Princetonne Center for Learning Disorders, the first independent private practise in occupational therapy in the U.S. She authored a clinical text published in 1980 on the treatment of children with learning disorders and holds a patent for inflatable equipment that helps learning-disordered children improve their balance. The American Occupational Therapy Association honoured Beskind as a Charter Fellow in recognition of her innovative therapeutic techniques. In 1989 she retyred after a 44-year career in occupational therapy.

Abstract:
Manufacturing labeled data for training deep learning models is costly and time-consuming, requiring extensive human effort and expertise. We introduce DatasetGAN, a lightweight framework for efficient, automated data collection and label synthesis. DatasetGAN minimizes human effort by using generative adversarial networks (GANs) to transform existing unlabeled data into realistic, labeled data. Our approach is fully automated, allowing users to select the desired data distribution and label space. We demonstrate effectiveness of our approach on three different datasets, showing that our method produces high-quality labeled data with minimal human effort. Our code and datasets are available at this https URL.

5 ("Cinq") [] is the fifth studio album by French recording artist Alizée. Originally scheduled for release in 2012, it was finally released on 25 March 2013 by Sony Music. Its music incorporates pop styles with elements of dance-pop, rock in the form of upbeat songs, piano-driven ballads and a renewed '60s style. The name and cover of the album were announced on 3 January 2013 on Star Academy. The first single to be released from the album was "À cause de l'automne" on 4 July 2012. The album was recorded in Paris and Brussels. With Alexandre Azaria as executive producer, she collaborated with a wide range of musicians, including many whom she had not collaborated with before, such as Thomas Boulard and BB Brunes. It debuted at 23rd place on the French Albums Chart, with first-week sales of 8000 copies. Internationally, the album charted moderately, marking career lows for Alizée. Two singles were released from the album. The first single, "À cause de l'automne" charted within the top 200 of the French-speaking countries. The second single, "Je veux bien", was released in April 2013.

In the small town ofPIPERSVILLE, awkward high school studenthalley swears she knows why she's gainedso much weight. She hasn't slept in months, andher metabolism has slowed. Something is hauntingher bedroom at night, and this entity steepedin malice appears to feed on her unrest.The longer it stays, the heavier Halley gets-andthe deeper she sinks into a turbulent sea ofpain, anger, and despair. By the time officerben cole arrives to investigate, the hauntinghas ripped Halley apart in every way possible.She's wracked with so much guilt and remorse,she's not sure how she can carry on. Benrecognizes the haunting for what it is, andhe's seen this before. He offers Halley asolution, but helping her will also meanentering the same terrifying darkness that nearlydevoured her.

Brian Moore thinks England's captain should not be a fullback. Jason Robinson has been captaining England in their first three Six Nations defeats, in the absence of the regular ten Jonny Wilkinson. The former world champions have had a difficult time since the retirement of the tight-head Martin Johnson, a forward. Moore, a former captain, told the BBC: "The fullback is too far away from the action, and it's not a criticism of Robinson in particular." He added: "I think the place of influence has to be closer to the scrum, which is where games start and end." Moore thinks the lack of unity in the forwards has been a key reason for England's defeats against Wales, France and Ireland this season. "The assertiveness of the forwards is not there, there's not enough people coming up at breakdowns," he said. "We're not getting quick ball and the backs are being restricted. Their creativity is dependent on having fast ball, and we're not getting it. " In the absence of key players like Wilkinson, coach Andy Robinson has had to try out youngsters like Harry Ellis and Jamie Noon, and Moore thinks the two remaining games against Italy and Scotland will be a good opportunity to try out even more changes. "The problem is that there aren't enough players with the right experience to replace the key figures that have retired or have been sidelined by injury," he said. "You can't do anything about it other than play them. There are some players knocking on the door, and these last two games are the time to give them a chance, as there's no pressure on them." Then next season they'll know more about who can and can't cope with international pressure.

Here is a basic recipe for crepes with strawberry sauce:

Ingredients:

- 2 eggs
- 1 cup all-purpose flour
- 1 cup milk
- 2 tablespoons butter, melted
- 1/4 teaspoon salt
- 1/4 teaspoon vanilla extract
- 1/4 teaspoon baking powder
- 1 tablespoon sugar
- 2 cups strawberries, sliced
- 2 tablespoons sugar, for strawberry sauce
- 2 tablespoons water, for strawberry sauce

Instructions:

1. In large bowl, whisk together eggs, flour, milk, butter, salt, vanilla, baking powder, and 1 tablespoon of sugar until smooth.
2. Heat a lightly greased skillet over medium-high heat.
3. Pour 1/4 cup of batter into the skillet, tilting the skillet to spread batter evenly.
4. Cook the crepe for about 2 minutes, until bottom is lightly browned.
5. Flip crepe over and cook for another minute.
6. Repeat with the remaining batter.
7. In a separate bowl, combine the strawberries, 2 tablespoons of sugar, and 2 tablespoons of water.
8. Heat strawberry mixture over medium heat, stirring occasionally, until sugar is dissolved and the strawberries are softened.
9. Serve the crepes with the strawberry sauce.

We present the studies of Sn/1-x/Cr/x/Te semimagnetic semiconductors with
chemical composition x ranging from 0.004 to 7.347. The structural
characterization indicates that even at low average Cr-content x < ?2.357, the
aggregation into micrometer size clusters appears in our samples. The magnetic
properties are affected by the presence of clusters. In all our samples we
observe the transition into the ordered state at temperatures between 895 and
064 K. The analysis of both static and dynamic magnetic susceptibility data
indicates that the spin-glass-like state is observed in our samples. The
addition of Cr to the alloy seems to shift the spin-glass-like transition from
374 K for x = 0.004 to 140 K for x = 0.012.

Rainwater harvesting is a method of collecting and storing rainwater for later use. It is an ancient practice that has been used for centuries in many parts of the world, and it is becoming increasingly popular as a way to conserve water and reduce the demand on freshwater resources.

The process of rainwater harvesting involves collecting rainwater from roofs, gutters, and other surfaces, and storing it in containers or tanks. The collected rainwater can then be used for various purposes, such as irrigation, washing, and even drinking.

Rainwater harvesting has several benefits, including reducing the demand on freshwater resources, conserving water, and reducing the risk of flooding. It can also help to improve the quality of the water by filtering it through the roof and other surfaces.

There are several ways to collect rainwater, including using roof gutters, downspouts, and other drainage systems, as well as using tanks, barrels, and other storage containers. The collected rainwater can be stored for later use or treated and used immediately.

Rainwater harvesting is an important tool for water conservation and management, and it has the potential to help reduce the demand on freshwater resources and improve the quality of the water.

As Sal and her mother prepare for their annual trip to Grandmother's house to pick blueberries, Sal tries on a special outfit that once belonged to her brother and jokes with her mother about how the shirt is too big and the pants are too long. The two of them take a shortcut through a thick forest, and Sal's mother warns her to be careful not to get lost. Sal gathers blueberries and fills her pail, but she accidentally wanders away from her mother and gets lost. She tries to follow her own footprints back, but they disappear when it starts to rain. 

She then encounters a mother bear and her cub, who offer to help her find her way home, and she follows them through the woods. Time passes, and Sal begins to realize that the bears are leading her in a circle. 

The cub tries to catch a frog, and Sal remembers that she left her pail of blueberries on the ground. When the bears go to take a nap, Sal leaves and returns to the spot where she left her pail. 

It is still there, so she picks it up and finds her way home. 

She is surprised to see her mother, who had been looking for her and was worried when she couldn't find her. 

Sal's mother welcomes her home and rewards her with a special blueberry pie.

Consider the normal Linear regression setup when the number of covariates p
is much larger than the Sample size n, and the covariates form correlated
Groups. The response variable y is not related to an entire group of covariates
in all or none basis, rather the Sparsity assumption persists within and
between groups. We extend the traditional g-prior setup to this framework.
Variable selection consistency of the proposed method is shown under fairly
general conditions, assuming the covariates to be random and allowing The true
model to grow with both n and p. For the purpose of implementation of the
proposed g-prior method to high-dimensional setup, we propose two procedures.
First, a group screening procedure, termed as group SIS (GSIS), and secondly, a
Novel stochastic search variable selection algorithm, termed as group informed
variable selection algorithm (GiVSA), which uses the known group structure
efficiently to explore the model space without discarding Any covariate based
on an initial screening. Screening consistency of GSIS, and theoretical mixing
time of GiVSA are Studied using the canonical path ensemble approach of Yang et
al. (2016). Performance of The proposed prior with implementation of GSIS as
well as GiVSA are validated using various simulated examples and a real data
related to residential Buildings.

THE IMPACT OF THE CONDITIONS OF STATIONARY TEMPERATURE AND STATIONARY HEATSOURCE ON ROTATIONAL CONVECTION IN FLAT GEOMETRY IN THE CLASS OF DESCRIPTIONS AND THE MEMORANDUM TO BURN. ENG. THE PIONEERING BOOK OF STATUTES, 1694; the arithmetic, according to the law of the Incommensurables, is based on the power of a charioteric surface. In this case, a double boundary layer with a dual structure is necessary, consisting of a classical Ekman layer in contact with the solid surfaces which adjusts the viscous stresses to zero, and a thermal wind-blanket layer, extending beyond the Ekman layer, which controls the temperature in such a way that the stationary heat-flux boundary conditions are satisfied. However, the impact of these boundary layers on the interior of the geostrophically balanced convection is shown to be asymptotically weak. After a simple rescaling of the thermal coefficients, the reduced system of the governing equations, the first-order system, is therefore identical for both boundary conditions. This means that a horizontal temperature change, which is decomposed into scales corresponding to the scale of the convection, has no effect at all, in the first order, on the interior convection.

US retail sales fell 0.3% in January, the biggest monthly decline since last August, driven down by a heavy fall in car sales.
The 3.3% fall in car sales had been expected, coming after December's 4% rise in car sales, fuelled by generous pre-Christmas special offers. Excluding the car sector, US retail sales were up 0.6% in January, twice what some analysts had been expecting. US retail spending is expected to rise in 2005, but not as quickly as in 2004.
Steve Gallagher, US chief economist at SG Corporate & Investment Banking, said January's figures were "decent numbers".
"We are not seeing the numbers that we saw in the second half of 2004, but they are still pretty healthy," he added. Sales at appliance and electronic stores were down 0.6% in January, while sales at hardware stores dropped by 0.3% and furniture store sales dipped 0.1%. Sales at clothing and clothing accessory stores jumped 1.8%, while sales at general merchandise stores, a category that includes department stores, rose by 0.9%. These strong gains were in part put down to consumers spending gift vouchers they had been given for Christmas.
Sales at restaurants, bars and coffee houses rose by 0.3%, while grocery store sales were up 0.5%. In December, overall retail sales rose by 1.1%. Excluding the car sector, sales rose by just 0.3%. Parul Jain, deputy chief economist at Nomura Securities International, said consumer spending would continue to rise in 2005, only at a slower rate of growth than in 2004. "Consumers continue to retain their strength in the first quarter," he said. Van Rourke, a bond strategist at Popular Securities, agreed that the latest retail sales figures were "slightly stronger than expected".

The chorus of criticism about the swelling number of festivals, tours, and ceremonies devoted to Hollywood has grown so loud that even the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences, which mounts the Oscars, has admitted to "awards fatigue." "There are too many award shows and too many awards,"Academy president David Rubin said last year. "It's confusing for the public."

In just the last decade, the number of ceremonies has multiplied, including the Oscars, the Emmys, the Grammys, the MTV Movie Awards, the Golden Globes, the Screen Actors Guild awards, and more. "We did not create enough opportunities during the year for [award shows] to matter as much as they should," Liz Garbus, producer of the Grammys, said earlier this year.

The boom reflects Hollywood's relentless hunt for new ways to promote its products and burnish its stars. But the glut of awards has begun to dilute the importance of each ceremony. The number of prime-time Emmy awards presented has more than doubled in the past decade, to over 100. The Oscar best-picture nominees jumped from five to ten in 2010, then returned to five in 2021. Movie studios have griped that the expansion diluted the value of the nomination. 

With so many awards shows on the calender, some salons have begun to feel like afterthoughts. The Academy Awards, for example, have lost some of their shine since their heyday in the 1990s. Only a quarter of this year's Oscars were watched, according to Nielsen data, down from a record 58.5 million viewers for the 1970 ceremony. By contrast, the 2023 Grammys scored just under 7 million viewers, down from nearly 19 million two decades ago.
