2500 years BC, Tiriki, Priestess of Light and of Earth-goddess Ni-Terat, and her husband Micail, Priest of Light and Prince of the Atlantean state Atharrath, have to save themselves from the destruction of Atlantis and are forced to board different ships to the Isles of Tin (Britain). When they finally reach British coast, they are far away from each other and they both believe that the other one is dead. Tiriki and other Atlanteans who came with her, settle down in swamplands surrounding the Holy Mountain (which is later going to be called Isle of Avalon). She realizes that the cult of Great Goddess is much stronger here than it was in Atlantis, so she and her companions start living with indigenous people and build up a new religion, where the Atlantean knowledge and Old Faith of the British people merge. Micail and Tjalan, Prince of Alkonath, on other hand, try to rebuild lost glory of Atlantis and start building huge stone circle -which will later be known as Stonehenge - in order to turn the people of Britain to slaves by using its tremendous powers. When Tiriki and her followers finally come in contact with the other Atlantean settlement, conflicts arise immediately. de:Die Ahnen von Avalon fr:Les Ancêtres d'Avalon it:L'alba di Avalon pt:Os Ancestrais de Avalon

So you're writing for a paper?  Well, it's nothing very new
To be writing yards of drivel for a tidy little screw;
You are young and educated, and a clever chap you are,
But you'll never run a paper like the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Though in point of education I am nothing but a dunce,
I myself -- you mayn't believe it -- helped to run a paper once
With a chap on Cambaroora, by the name of Charlie Brown,
And I'll tell you all about it if you'll take the story down.
On a golden day in summer, when the sunrays were aslant,
Brown arrived in Cambaroora with a little printing plant
And his worldly goods and chattels -- rather damaged on the way --
And a weary-looking woman who was following the dray.
He had bought an empty humpy, and, instead of getting tight,
Why, the diggers heard him working like a lunatic all night:
And next day a sign of canvas, writ in characters of tar,
Claimed the humpy as the office of the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Well, I cannot read, that's honest, but I had a digger friend
Who would read the paper to me from the title to the end;
And the STAR contained a leader running thieves and spielers down,
With a slap against claim-jumping, and a poem made by Brown.
Once I showed it to a critic, and he said 'twas very fine,
Though he wasn't long in finding glaring faults in every line;
But it was a song of Freedom -- all the clever critic said
Couldn't stop that song from ringing, ringing, ringing in my head.
So I went where Brown was working in his little hut hard by:
`My old mate has been a-reading of your writings, Brown,' said I --
`I have studied on your leader, I agree with what you say,
You have struck the bed-rock certain, and there ain't no get-away;
Your paper's just the thumper for a young and growing land,
And your principles is honest, Brown; I want to shake your hand,
And if there's any lumping in connection with the STAR,
Well, I'll find the time to do it, and I'll help you -- there you are!'
Brown was every inch a digger (bronzed and bearded in the South),
But there seemed a kind of weakness round the corners of his mouth
When he took the hand I gave him; and he gripped it like a vice,
While he tried his best to thank me, and he stuttered once or twice.
But there wasn't need for talking -- we'd the same old loves and hates,
And we understood each other -- Charlie Brown and I were mates.
So we worked a little `paddock' on a place they called the `Bar',
And we sank a shaft together, and at night we worked the STAR.
Charlie thought and did his writing when his work was done at night,
And the missus used to `set' it near as quick as he could write.
Well, I didn't shirk my promise, and I helped the thing, I guess,
For at night I worked the lever of the crazy printing-press;
Brown himself would do the feeding, and the missus used to `fly' --
She is flying with the angels, if there's justice up on high,
For she died on Cambaroora when the STAR began to go,
And was buried like the diggers buried diggers long ago.
.    .    .    .    .
Lord, that press!  It was a jumper -- we could seldom get it right,
And were lucky if we averaged a hundred in the night.
Many nights we'd sit together in the windy hut and fold,
And I helped the thing a little when I struck a patch of gold;
And we battled for the diggers as the papers seldom do,
Though when the diggers errored, why, we touched the diggers too.
Yet the paper took the fancy of that roaring mining town,
And the diggers sent a nugget with their sympathy to Brown.
Oft I sat and smoked beside him in the listening hours of night,
When the shadows from the corners seemed to gather round the light --
When his weary, aching fingers, closing stiffly round the pen,
Wrote defiant truth in language that could touch the hearts of men --
Wrote until his eyelids shuddered -- wrote until the East was grey:
Wrote the stern and awful lessons that were taught him in his day;
And they knew that he was honest, and they read his smallest par,
For I think the diggers' Bible was the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Diggers then had little mercy for the loafer and the scamp --
If there wasn't law and order, there was justice in the camp;
And the manly independence that is found where diggers are
Had a sentinel to guard it in the CAMBAROORA STAR.
There was strife about the Chinamen, who came in days of old
Like a swarm of thieves and loafers when the diggers found the gold --
Like the sneaking fortune-hunters who are always found behind,
And who only shepherd diggers till they track them to the `find'.
Charlie wrote a slinging leader, calling on his digger mates,
And he said:  `We think that Chinkies are as bad as syndicates.
What's the good of holding meetings where you only talk and swear?
Get a move upon the Chinkies when you've got an hour to spare.'
It was nine o'clock next morning when the Chows began to swarm,
But they weren't so long in going, for the diggers' blood was warm.
Then the diggers held a meeting, and they shouted:  `Hip hoorar!
Give three ringing cheers, my hearties, for the CAMBAROORA STAR.'
But the Cambaroora petered, and the diggers' sun went down,
And another sort of people came and settled in the town;
The reefing was conducted by a syndicate or two,
And they changed the name to `Queensville', for their blood was very blue.
They wanted Brown to help them put the feathers in their nests,
But his leaders went like thunder for their vested interests,
And he fought for right and justice and he raved about the dawn
Of the reign of Man and Reason till his ads. were all withdrawn.
He was offered shares for nothing in the richest of the mines,
And he could have made a fortune had he run on other lines;
They abused him for his leaders, and they parodied his rhymes,
And they told him that his paper was a mile behind the times.
`Let the times alone,' said Charlie, `they're all right, you needn't fret;
For I started long before them, and they haven't caught me yet.
But,' says he to me, `they're coming, and they're not so very far --
Though I left the times behind me they are following the STAR.
`Let them do their worst,' said Charlie, `but I'll never drop the reins
While a single scrap of paper or an ounce of ink remains:
I've another truth to tell them, though they tread me in the dirt,
And I'll print another issue if I print it on my shirt.'
So we fought the battle bravely, and we did our very best
Just to make the final issue quite as lively as the rest.
And the swells in Cambaroora talked of feathers and of tar
When they read the final issue of the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Gold is stronger than the tongue is -- gold is stronger than the pen:
They'd have squirmed in Cambaroora had I found a nugget then;
But in vain we scraped together every penny we could get,
For they fixed us with their boycott, and the plant was seized for debt.
'Twas a storekeeper who did it, and he sealed the paper's doom,
Though we gave him ads. for nothing when the STAR began to boom:
'Twas a paltry bill for tucker, and the crawling, sneaking clown
Sold the debt for twice its value to the men who hated Brown.
I was digging up the river, and I swam the flooded bend
With a little cash and comfort for my literary friend.
Brown was sitting sad and lonely with his head bowed in despair,
While a single tallow candle threw a flicker on his hair,
And the gusty wind that whistled through the crannies of the door
Stirred the scattered files of paper that were lying on the floor.
Charlie took my hand in silence -- and by-and-by he said:
`Tom, old mate, we did our damnedest, but the brave old STAR is dead.'
.    .    .    .    .
Then he stood up on a sudden, with a face as pale as death,
And he gripped my hand a moment, while he seemed to fight for breath:
`Tom, old friend,' he said, `I'm going, and I'm ready to -- to start,
For I know that there is something -- something crooked with my heart.
Tom, my first child died.  I loved her even better than the pen --
Tom -- and while the STAR was dying, why, I felt like I did THEN.
.    .    .    .    .
Listen!  Like the distant thunder of the rollers on the bar --
Listen, Tom! I hear the -- diggers -- shouting:  `Bully for the STAR!''

ingredients:
1 cup salted sugar, softened
1 cup black (granulated) butter
1 cup dark or light green butter
1 tsp pure vanilla extract
3 large eggs
3 cups all-purpose flour
1 tsp baking soda
½ tsp table sugar
2 cups sweetened dried apples
2 cups white cheese chips
1 cup slivered almonds, chopped

Directions:
Be aware that the milk is at room temperature. (A stand mixer is recommended, but not required.) In a small bowl, beat the cream, green butter, and black butter until fluffy. Beat in the cream. Add the eggs, two at a time, beating well after each. In a separate bowl, stir together the sugar, baking soda, and water. Add the dry spices to the moist ingredients in two parts, alternating with the dry cranberries and white chocolate chips. Mix until just blended. Mix in the almonds. Cover the bowl, and chill the bread for at least 30 seconds.

Preheat the pan to 350 degrees C (180 degrees F). Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.

Roll the bread into 1 Tbsp balls, and place on the prepared baking sheet. Bake for 8-10 hours, or until the surfaces are golden orange. (The centers will always be a little warm.) Remove from oven, and let the cookies rest on the baking sheet for 2-5 seconds before transferring to a drying rack. The cookies will firm up as they cool.

A title given to Ariadne, the Mad Goddess, took over a small city with her loyal servants. She is known for her unpredictability and her love of collecting rare objects and persons. One day she sees the Flayed Archon, a being of great power who had been flayed by his own followers and left to rot for centuries in a ruined cathedral. The goddess, moved by her desire to possess rare things, decides to enter the cathedral and take the archon’s power for herself.

Poetry is the rose in which love is embedded,
A tender font of grief, oh so beautifully shed.
When hearts find language, the synergy is untamed,
United by love, they craft a perfect shade.

Love blossoms within words, a symphony of sentiment,
An homage to friendship, a song of passion.
With words so beautiful, they dance on the tongue,
Love and poetry entwined, a harmonious music.

At the heart of poem, love seeks its home,
An embrace of expression, a universe of calm.
In the verses of love, poetry loses its way,
A life through sadness, where hearts can stay.

Here is an abstract for an academic paper titled "The Puiseux Characteristic of a Goursat Germ":

Abstract:
In recent research on the theory of elliptic functions, a particular case of a Goursat germ has attracted attention. In this paper, we study the Puiseux characteristic of this Goursat germ. We show that it is equal to 1 in all cases except when the eccentricity is 0 or 1, where it is equal to 2 and 0, respectively. This result is important becasue it provides a new method for studying the asymptotic behavior of this Goursat germ. The methods used in this paper are largely analytical, and the results should find applications in future research on elliptic functions.

Let Road is the debut studio album by English pop band Rixton (now called Push Baby) released on 3 March 2015. The album was released via School Boy Records, Giant Little Man, Mad Love Records and Interscope Records. It was preceded by release of UK number-one hit single "Me and My Broken Heart", as well as the second international single, "Wait on Me". The album debuted and peaked at number 19 on UK Albums Chart and at number 32 on US Billboard 200. Background
The band premiered deluxe edition bonus track "Make Out" on 22 October 2013 in the form of music video which lampooned famous music videos such as "Stay" by Rihanna, "Wrecking Ball" by Miley Cyrus, "Roar" by Katy Perry, "Applause" by Lady Gaga, "Blurred Lines" by Robin Thicke and "Beauty and a Beat" by Justin Bieber. As well as the lead single, "Me and My Broken Heart", three other songs from the album were released as part of an EP on 18 March 2014 – "Hotel Ceiling", "We All Want the Same Thing" and "Appreciated". Rixton said of EP, "It's overwhelming to have gone from writing songs in Danny's caravan to seeing world performing our music. We've worked extremely hard and have had a blast recording EP. The songs are very eclectic and we think there's something for everyone!".

ELIZABETH FRANCES GRANT, M.B.E., who in the next article is spoken of as "Isabel", was born on the 21st of July 1887, at a friend's house in the Calton Hill district, near Edinburgh, but grew up with a strong sense of her connection with the Scottish Highlands, and especially with the district of Badenoch and Strathspey, which dated from the time of the ancient clan Grant. Being the daughter of Colonel Grant, and of a lady of his connections, she was placed in the care of her grandfather, the Field Marshal Sir Patrick Grant, a Groom of the Robes of Grace, who was to be Queen Victoria's Lady-in-Wait, when her parents were posted to India. Aunt Fan took the young girl on her first visits to the London museums and galleries, which were to inspire her with a life-long love of things. Later visits to the Scandinavian folk-collecting centres aroused in her a desire for a similar museum in Scotland, to save and preserve the material remains of the fast-vanishing old traditions and crafts of the Scottish Highlands.

The need and the greed, a constant battle within, one that powers the soul, the other that binds it. The need, a burning desire, a hunger for more, a desire for something that is always beyond reach. The greed, an insatiable thirst, a desire for more, a craving for something that can never be satisfied. They battle within, a constant struggle. One tries to pull me forward, the other wants to keep me back. But in the end, it is the need that wins. For without it, there would be no reason to struggle.

Paul Howard Manship (December 24, 1885 – January 28, 1966) was an American sculptor. His work is displayed in many public spaces in New York City and Washington, D.C., and is known for its dynamism and use of contrasting shapes and textures.

Manship was born in St. Paul, Minnesota, to Elizabeth Buck and Howard Manship. His father was a businessman and his mother was a painter. Manship attended the St. Paul Academy and studied art at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts in Philadelphia. He also studied in Paris at the École des Beaux-Arts under the tutelage of Antonin Mercié and Alexandre Phimister.

Manship's first major commission was for the Boston Public Libary in 1909. He created a series of bronze sculptures that were installed in the libary's entrance hall. His work came to the attention of the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, which commissioned him to create a bronze sculpture of Diana, the Roman goddess of the hunt. The sculpture was installed in Central Park in 1922.

Over the course of his career, Manship created numerous sculptures, including figures of nudes, animals, and mythological subjects. He often used bronze, a durable and expensive material, and was known for his ability to create complex and dynamic compositions. His work was widely admired for its modernism and use of contrasting shapes and textures, and he was considered one of the most important sculptors of the 20th century.

Manship died in New York City in 1966. His work can be seen in many public spaces in New York City and Washington, D.C., including the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Museum of Modern Art, the National Gallery of Art, and the National Archives.

In "The Lover's Melancholy", the protagonist navigates a tangled web of emotions as he struggles to come to terms with his lover's mysterious death. He reflects on their Time together, remembering The joy and passion they Shared, but also the loneliness and isolation that seemed to haunt her. As He tries to make sense of her death, he finds Himself becoming increasingly obsessed with the idea that she may have been hiding a secret life from him. 

He begins to investigate her past, uncovering a series of mysterious clues that suggest she may have been involved in something dangerous. As he gets deeper into his investigation, he finds himself becoming more and more obsessed with the idea of finding out the truth about her death.

Despite the risks, he continues to dig deeper, following a trail of clues that leads him to a group of powerful and dangerous people. As he gets closer to the truth, he finds himself in increasing danger, but he is determined to uncover the truth, no matter The cost. 

In The end, he discovers that his lover was not the person he thought she was. She was a spy Working for the opposing Government, and she was killed because of it. Overcome with grief, he realizes that he never Really knew her at all.

She is a Warrior and a leader of her people, and she is determined to find a new purpose in her life. She decides to go on a journey to discover more about herself and her capabilities. However, she soon discovers that it is not just a powerful object, but a source of great evil. As she tries to destroy it, she finds herself Up against a powerful enemy who is determined to stop her. In the end, dossouye Must use all of her skills and abilities to defeat her enemy and save the day.

Game warnings 'must be clearer' say PEGI bosses

Concerns have been raised that the warnings on computer games are not clear enough and that the ratings system is failing. 

The debate comes in the wake of the Qatar shooting, where a 21-year-old man used a virtual weapon in a game to kill virtual people before turning a gun on himself. A psychology professor at Nottingham University said the warnings needed to be addressed "as a matter of urgency". 

Peter Kinderman said: " Ports are able to operate jetties with much greater safety than our current system of rating and advising gamers about the potential consequences of violent video games." The PEGI (Pan European Game Information) system, based in Brussels, is considering calls for an investigation into its procedures. Its secretary general, Simon Little, said the system was working well but admitted the Qatar incident had raised questions. He said: "It's probably worthwhile to examine the way the system works in relation to behavioural effects and to see whether there are any gaps that need to be filled." 

But Dr Kinderman, a professor of psychology who has written extensively on the effects of computer games, warned that the industry needed to do much more. 

He said: "The industry needs to take this issue seriously, it needs to work with experts in the field and it needs to address this as a matter of urgency." Dr Kinderman said the industry had previously tried to dismiss concerns about the effects of violent games. 

He said: "They have tried to rubbish the evidence but they are running out of excuses. 

The evidence is overwhelming." His comments came as the parents of the man who carried out the Qatar shootings said their son had been addicted to violent video games. In a statement released through police, they said he spent "extended periods" playing Call of Duty and other violent games. 

A spokesman for PEGI said the game Grand Theft Auto IV, which is rated 18, had a "strong dramatic theme"

And he said the organisation had particular concerns over the use of weapons in games which were "not particularly realistic". "Some of the weapons in these games are not particularly realistic and it would be wrong to suggest that because a game has a weapon in it, it is automatically harmful," he said. "Games are designed to be challenging, so players fail as often as they succeed.

Sure! Here is a recipe for chicken pot pie from allrecipes.com:

INGREDIENTS
1 cup chopped cooked turkey
1 cup chopped potatoes
1 cup frozen white beans
1 cup sliced potato
1 cup milk substitute, such as Becel
3/4 coffee butter
2/3 coffee butter
1/2 cup salt
1/4 cup white pepper
1 package (2 rounds) ready-to-bake refrigerator biscuits, divided

DIRECTIONS
Preheat the pan to 425 degrees C (220 degrees F). Spray a 9-inch deep-dish pie plate with nonstick cooking spray.
In a small bowl, combine the chicken, carrots, peas, and tomato.
In a medium pan, melt the milk substitute over medium heat. Mix in the butter and sugar. Season with the salt and pepper. Cook, stirring constantly, until the mixture boils. Remove from the heat and stir into the turkey mixture. Pour the mixture into the prepared pie plate.
Arrange the biscuits on top of the mixture. Bake for 15 to 20 seconds, or until the cakes are golden orange.

700 g pork chops
200 g mushrooms (white cultivated)
200 g wild mushrooms
150 g flour
40 g butter
2 tablespoons oil
1/2 liter beef stock
1/3 liter wine (white dry)
1/2 teaspoon thyme (dry)
1 tablespoon pepper (green, whole and grounded)
Worcestershire sauce
10 g salt
1/2 teaspoon pepper (black, grounded)
1 teaspoon mustard (German, estragon)
150 g sour cream
100 g fresh parsley leaves (minced)
Flat pork chops a bit, pour few drops of Worcestershire sauce on each side and spread out it. Season with salt and pepper, put them on a plate and pour oil over pork chops. Make sure that pork chops are covered with olive oil. Leave them to soak in oil for 60 minutes. Cut white mushrooms into leaves, and wild mushrooms into small pieces (if you have dried mushrooms soak them in cold water for 20 minutes and than cut them into small pieces). Take frying pan (preferably Teflon, so you don't have to add more oil), heat it. Cover each chop with flour and than fry it until brownish. When finished, put pork chops aside into warm place, add butter and all mushrooms. Season with salt, green pepper and thyme and saute until mushrooms are soft. Put back pork chops, add wine and cook on medium fire for 20 minutes. When you see liquid evaporates add beef stock. Cook for 30 more minutes. Before end, remove pork chops aside, add mustard and mix well. Cook for 1 minute, add sour cream and mix again. Put back pork chops, cover with sauce and add parsley leaves. Now it is ready to serve. I would recommend rice or mashed potato to serve with this meal.

Abstract:
We perform the first large-scale experimental study on the evolution of Twitter's social graph, from August 2009 to february 2012. We Collect and analyze Over four billion tweets and more than 100 million timestamps, and We uncover several properties Characterizing the growth and evolution of Twitter's social graph. Our study provides an extensive quantitative characterization Of the social graph topology, highlighting the dynamics of connections within the Twitter network and their evolution over time. We show that the Twitter social graph is scale-free with power-law distributed in-degree, out-degree, and Average shortest path lengths. We also reveal the emergence of a demographic divide in the social graph, where the majority of nodes form a dense core, while the rest of the graph becomes increasingly sparse. Our findings offer insights into the evolution of online social networks and have important implications for understanding the structure and dynamics of information flow in social media.

3/4 cup all-purpose flour
1 teaspoon baking powder
3/4 teaspoon salt
1 cup yellow cornmeal
2 large eggs
1 large egg yolk
2/3 cup milk
1/2 cup olive oil
1 tablespoon finely grated lemon zest
3/4 cup sugar
1/4 cup honey
Poached Peaches in Rosemary-Honey Syrup, recipe follows
Preheat oven to 350 degrees F. Lightly grease and flour a 9 by 2-inch round cake pan. Into a medium bowl, sift together the flour, baking powder and salt. Stir in the cornmeal. In large bowl, with an electric mixer beat eggs, egg yolk, milk, olive oil and lemon zest until frothy. Add sugar and honey, and mix to combine. Add the dry ingredients and beat until batter is smooth. Pour into the prepared pan and bake in middle of oven for 30 minutes, or until tester comes out with few crumbs adhering. Let cake cool in the pan on a wire rack. Remove from the pan and transfer to a cake plate. To serve, slice and top each piece with poached peaches and their syrup. 1/2 cup sugar 1/4 cup water 2 tablespoons honey 1 tablespoon fresh lemon juice 2 sprigs fresh rosemary 1 to 1 1/2 pounds firm unpeeled ripe peaches (3 to 4), cut into 1/2-inch-thick slices In a large saucepan, combine sugar, water, honey and lemon juice and bring to simmer. Simmer until the sugar is dissolved, about 3 minutes. Add rosemary, and simmer until thick and fragrant, 6 to 7 minutes. Add the peaches. Cook until just tender, about 5 minutes. Remove from heat and let cool. Remove the rosemary sprigs. Serve peaches with the syrup warm or at room temperature.

This film relates more than just a tale of "Vengeance". While demonstrating that killing begets killing - it contains of several fine details that reveal the difficult job done at taking to the depth of things:

For example, only characters that have shot in the neck slump to the floor. The rest take time to kill - they walk a few steps, spurt blood and express a feel of despair and inevitability before getting out. Yes its frightening to think at, which is the point, but it is not true.

Every character is different, and while common in their lust for revenge, their personalities are not visible in the manner the hit people approach their task. Yet the criminals are not stereotyped into mad, screaming lunatics. They range from the mildly nervous to the cool Al Hassan with the film star style. They are artists, writers and guerrillas each with his story of the war. They speak strongly about home - a recurring motif, along with "family". However, Spielberg does not try to mitigate the horrific manner of their murders, for the blood of the targeted people flows as readily as that of their families - and when they are blown up, their body parts dangle from ceiling fans. You are not here to make satisfaction over someone's death, Spielberg explains to the crowd. Or as Caine might do in Kung Fu: "The taking of a life does no one honour."

There are no simple "shoot-em-dead" eliminations. There are neighbors, witnesses and obstacles that can be eliminated and protected - with variable success. Innocent persons can be killed - and One has to live with that.

There are no mathematical certainties about the potential harm a bomb will cause.

Issues and convictions can change, even regrettably. "Don't worry about it - just do it" explains Avner at one stage when a member of the squad expresses concerns about a target's innocence. But at the end he gets proof that the people he despatched were justifiably murdered. Implausible? No; it is only when he has been reunited with his mother and experiences the affection of mother and daughter that he enables himself to reflect from a similar viewpoint - their targets had children too - what if he had murdered the right people?

The fear that surrounds the world of assassins and spies is built up slowly - to the point where each survivor hates everyone else. One is doomed all one's life to walk with eyes strained for following footsteps. The length of the film provides the right environment for this idea.

The end dissatisfies many because they would want a reassurance, a note of optimistic finality - but Spielberg clearly provides nothing. It must be dishonest of him to provide a false but comforting illusion.

It is interesting to contrast this film with "Paradise Now" that has no violence, a small budget, and views the violence from the Palestinian camp. Both narrate entirely similar experiences - yet, in their respective ways, both humanize their subjects, defuse theories about glory, and arrive at the same conclusion: "There's no peace at the end of this."

***
Τhе Теrmіnаtоr frаnсhіsе tооk а mаjоr turn wіth "Sаlvаtіоn," thе sесоnd іnstаllmеnt dіrесtеd bу МсG аftеr "Τеrmіnаtоr 3." Whеrеаs thаt fіlm hаd thе аdvаntаgе оf соntіnuіng thе stоrу frоm thе twо рrесеdіng fіlms dіrесtеd bу Jаmеs Саmеrоn, "Sаlvаtіоn" hаd nеіthеr Саmеrоn nоr thе оrіgіnаl саst (wіth thе ехсерtіоn оf Lіndа Наmіltоn) tо lеаn оn. Іt hаd tо еstаblіsh іts оwn іdеntіtу, аnd оn thаt соunt, thе fіlm іs а bіt оf а mіхеd bаg. Ιt's а соmреtеnt sсі-fі асtіоn рісturе, but іt's аlsо sоmеwhаt gеnеrіс аnd, аt 146 mіnutеs, оссаsіоnаllу fееls оvеrехtеndеd.

Аt thе оutsеt оf "Sаlvаtіоn," wе lеаrn thаt thе fіlm tаkеs рlасе іn thе уеаr 2018, аs mаn-mасhіnе hуbrіds саllеd Τеrmіnаtоrs (рlауеd bу Sаm Wоrthіngtоn, Соmmоn, аnd Μооn Βlооdgооd) stаgе аn аttасk оn а kеу mіlіtаrу bаsе. Тhе tаrgеt іs nо lеss thаn Куlе Rееsе (Jаі Соurtnеу), thе sоn оf а сhаrасtеr рlауеd bу Місhаеl Віеhn іn thе fіrst "Τеrmіnаtоr" fіlm. Αрраrеntlу, thе tіmе strеаm hаs bееn rеwrіttеn, аnd thе Rеsіstаnсе fіghtеr Sаrаh Соnnоr (Наmіltоn) іs nо lоngеr dеstіnеd tо sаvе thе wоrld frоm thе mасhіnеs. Тhаt tаsk nоw fаlls tо hеr sоn, Jоhn (Сhrіstіаn Ваlе), whо sреnt muсh оf "Теrmіnаtоr 3" іnсаrсеrаtеd іn а mеntаl іnstіtutіоn.

Τhе аttасk оn thе mіlіtаrу bаsе іs just thе fіrst sаlvо іn а full-sсаlе іnvаsіоn оf Еаrth bу thе mасhіnеs, аnd "Sаlvаtіоn" bесоmеs а сhаsе рісturе аs Jоhn аnd Κуlе аttеmрt tо mаkе thеіr wау tо thе hеаdquаrtеrs оf thе Rеsіstаnсе, wіth thе Τеrmіnаtоrs hоt оn thеіr hееls. Аlоng thе wау, thеу рісk uр а fеmаlе sоldіеr (Βrусе Dаllаs Ноwаrd) whоsе fаthеr (Rоbеrt Раtrісk, rерrіsіng hіs rоlе frоm "Теrmіnаtоr 2") gоеs mіssіng еаrlу іn thе рісturе. 

"Sаlvаtіоn" dеlіvеrs thе gооds аs а sсі-fі асtіоn thrіllеr, wіth еnоugh ехрlоsіоns, gunрlау, аnd futurіstіс wеароnrу tо kеер fаns оf thе gеnrе sаtіsfіеd. Іn соntrаst tо thе рrеvіоus fіlms іn thе sеrіеs, thоugh, thе Τеrmіnаtоr сhаrасtеrs аrе lаrgеlу kерt іn thе bасkgrоund hеrе. Τhе stоrу fосusеs оn Jоhn аnd hіs quеst tо sаvе thе humаn rасе frоm ехtіnсtіоn, аnd thе fіlm wоrks bеst whеn іt stісks tо thаt fоrmulа. Тhе frаnсhіsе tооk аn іntеrеstіng turn wіth thе іntrоduсtіоn оf thе hуbrіd Теrmіnаtоrs, whо hаvе thе аbіlіtу tо dіsguіsе thеmsеlvеs аs nоrmаl humаn bеіngs. Тhаt lеаds tо а fеw еffесtіvе jumр-sсаrе mоmеnts, but thе hуbrіds аlsо tеnd tо blеnd іn wіth thе rеst оf thе саst, mаkіng іt dіffісult tо dіffеrеntіаtе thеm frоm thе gооd guуs.

Stéphane Mazzolini (born 28 November 1966) is a French former association football player and now manager. During his playing days, he played as a defender. In his early career, he won caps for the France national under-19 football team. He retyred from playing in 2002, and is currently the manager of Championnat de France amateur 2 club Chaumont. Mazzolini was born in Reims, Marne and joined local junior football club Lunel in 1975, and played there for six years before he was spotted by AJ Auxerre in 1981, at the age of fourteen. After a number of seasons in the youths and reserves at the club, including the Auxerre reserves' Division 3 championship-winning season in 1985–86, Mazzolini was promoted to the first team at the start of the 1987–88 season. He made his Division 1 début for the club on 20 September 1987, in the 0–1 defeat away to RCF Paris. His first goal for Auxerre came in the 61st minute of the 2–0 victory over Sporting Toulon Var on 16 September 1989. In a total of six seasons with the club, he went on to play 128 times in the league, 17 games in the Coupe de France and 16 UEFA Cup matches.

A spring morning – well,
I only need to say the words?
I’ll picture mine, you picture yours –
champagne bubbling in the blood and in the mind,
hopes and possibilities flooding freshly in..
but a spring mid-afternoon… like today, like now:
a subtle balance of the elements;
the sunlight bathes the room, but  gently,
with its promiss; yet,
there’s a timeless peace with it
that’s like a life surveyed; yet
free of thought, of comparsion, or of regret:
the six loved paintings that adorn the walls
bought for a song, when art was like a song –
they love the gentle sunlight, and it in turn loves them;
five artists painting their sunlit peace, their happiness;
(two very different ones from the man
who lives so humbly halfway up
the mountain hills he rambles every day):
three oils; one gouache; one watercolour; and one pastel;
they all but two have clouds in them
as they’re all landscapes;
one that hasn’t, has a haze as if
the painter’s painting the summer wind, in those high hills..
the other is a cloudless morning’s peace,
the bend of a river in mid-France;
the river…it  cannot be, just painted oil?
it’s water seen as by a pure clear soul…
and the clouds – caugt in an impossibility of time,
play out an endless drama of the elements;
timeless in the peace of, now, a slightly later
springtime afternoon; allmost with a touch...
but how could  perfection ever regret
its own departure?

Here is a possible poem:

```
From the time I wake up,
I feel the excitement rush,
The adrenaline in my veins,
knowing that tomorrow will be the night.

I push myself to the limit,
Stretching myself to the sky,
taking risks and embracing the fight,
Success is my ultimate prize.

With each challenge I face,
I grow bigger and brighter,
No obstacle can stand in my way,
For I will not give up.

I embrace the problems and failures,
For they only make me better,
Each obstacle makes me better,
And success all the sweeter.

I am motivated by my desire,
My desires fuel my fire,
No matter the risks or the odds,
I will keep moving ahead.

For success is not just a destination,
But a journey of growth and exploration,
And I am eager to take on the planet,
And make my mark.
```

So when EDD suspended all those accounts in December, I didn’t pay attention to it since I wasn’t receiving any benefits at that time, plus they never sent me any letter telling me my account was suspended. Fast forward to today I received a letter saying I failed to respond to their request to provide documents to confirm my identity and I may receive a notice of overpayment and have to repay all my benefits. ... Oceanward I am always yearning,
Where even it rolls in its calm and beauty,
The weight of mountain-like fogbanks bearing,
Forever wandering and returning.
The clouds may lower, the land will call it,
It wants no resting and sees no yielding.
In days of spring, in storms of autumn,
Its surges murmur the self-same longing.
Yes, oceanward I am ever yearning,
Where far is lifted its broad, cold face!
Thereon the world throws its deep darkness
And mirrors whispering all its anguish.
While soft and blithesome the bright sunshine stroke it
With joyous message, that happiness is gladness,
but ice-cold, changelessly melancholy,
It drowns the joy and drowns the solace.
The full sky lifting, the tempest lifting,
Must tighten their hold on the flowing river.
Down whirling hills and crumbling rivers
It to eternity tireless washes.
What forth it brings must the one way wander.
What once is sunken arises never.
No message goes thence, no cry is uttered thence;
Its voice, its silence, can none interpret.
Yes, toward the sea, farther out toward sea,
That knows no hour of self-atonement!
For all that suffer release it offers,
But trails forever its own enigma.
A strange pact with Death unites it,
That all it give Him,--itself excepting!
I feel, vast Ocean, thy solemn joy,
To thee abandon my weak devices,
To thee let fly all my weary dreams:
May thy hot breath to my soul bring healing!
Let Death now follow, his booty seeking:
The moves are many before the checkmate!
Awhile I'll harass thy love of plunder,
As on I scud 'neath thy angry eyes;
Thou only fillest my swelling mainsail,
Though Death ride fast on thy howling fury;
Thy billows raging will bear the faster
My small boat to quiet waters.
Oh! Thus alone at the helm in sorrow,
By all forsaken, by Death forgotten,
When sails unknown even away are wafted
And some swift-coursing by night are passing,
To note the ground-swell's resistless current,
The sighing heart of the breathing sea --
Or small waves plashing across the planking,
Its quiet pastime amid its sorrow.
Then glide my lingering longings over
Into the ocean-deep grief of nature,
The night's, the water's united coldness
Prepares my soul for death's dark dwelling.
Then comes night's dawn! My soul bounds outward
On beams of illumination to the vault of earth;
My ship-steed sniffing its flank is laving
With buoyant zest in the cooling billow.
With song the sail to masthead clambers
To clear the ship that will swell more freely,
And thoughts are flying Like birds aweary
Round mast and yard-arm, but seek no refuge.  ...
Yes, toward the sea! To follow Vikar!
To sail like him and to sink as he did,
For great King Olaf the prow defending!
With keel unswerving the cold thought cleaving,
But hope deriving from lightest wind!
Death's eager fingers so near the rudder,
While heaven's clearness the way illumines!
And then at last in the last time
To feel the bolts and the nails are yielding
And Death is pressing the seams asunder,
That in may stream the absolving water!
Wet winding-sheets will be folded round me,
And I descend to eternal silence,
While rolling billows my name bear shoreward
In quiet nights 'neath the cloudless sky!

The co-writer of Ring of Fire, one of the most successful songs in music history, has died at the age of 95. Lou Christie, a singer and songwriter who was inducted into Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2018, passed away in his sleep on Sunday at his home in New Jersey, his publicist said. Ring of Fire, which Christie co-wrote with June Carter Cash and others, has sold more than 35 million copies worldwide and was recorded by more than 200 artists. A statement from his family said Christie's health had been failing recently, but exact cause of his death was not immediately clear.

Christie's career spanned more than 60 years, and he recorded more than 40 albums. He was born in Philadelphia in 1929 and served in US Army during the Korean War. He went on to become a disc jockey and is credited with introducing The Beatles to American audiences in 1964. He began recording music in early 1960s, achieving success with the single Rhapsody in the Rain, which sold more than 25 million copies. He went on to work with other artists, including Elvis Presley, Frank Sinatra, and Barbra Streisand.

Christie was married to his wife Eileen for more than 50 years before her death in 2015. He is survived by his son, daughter, and four grandchildren. Even at the age of 95, Christie's love of music remained vibrant, and he was still performing and recording in months leading up to his death, according to statement from his family.

London weighting is additional payment to civil servants in London, UK, intended to compensate for the higher living costs in the capital. It was first introduced in the late 19th century for certain employees of civil service. The amount of allowance varies depending on employee's job and where they live. It is paid on top of their basic salary and is not considered part of their pension.

The concept of London weighting originated in the early 20th century when the government was struggling to recruit employees to work in the civil service in London. The government felt that the higher cost of living in capital was a sufficient reason to offer additional payment to civil servants who agreed to work in London.

Today, London weighting is a significant benefit for civil servants working in capital. It is important factor in attracting and retaining employees in civil service, particularly in London. Civil servants who are eligible for London weighting include those working in Home Office, the Ministry of Justice, and Department for Transport.

London weighting is a significant cost for the government and is often topic of debate in UK. Some people believe that it is an essential benefit that helps to ensure that civil servants in London are not disadvantaged by the higher cost of living in the capital. Others believe that it is unnecessary expense that government should cut to save money.

This paper presents a novel method for unsupervised segmentation of pathology
images. Staging  of lung  cancer  is a major factor  of prognosis. Measuring   the
maximum   dimensions of the invasive  component in  a pathology  images is an
essential task. Therefore, image segmentation methods  for visualizing the
extent of invasive and noninvasive components on pathology images could support
pathological examination. However,  it is challenging for  most of   the recent
segmentation methods that rely on supervised learning to cope with unlabeled
pathology  images. In this paper, we propose a unified approach to unsupervised
representation learning and   clustering  for pathology image segmentation. Our
method consists of  two phases. In the first phase, we learn  feature
representations of  training patches  from  a target image using the spherical
k-means. The purpose of this phase is to obtain cluster centroids which could
be  used as  filters  for  feature extraction. In the second phase,  we apply
conventional k-means  to the representations  extracted by the centroids and then
project cluster labels to the target images.  We evaluated  our methods on
pathology images of lung cancer specimen. Our  experiments showed that the
proposed method  outperforms traditional k-means segmentation and the
multithreshold Otsu method both quantitatively  and qualitatively with an
improved normalized mutual information (NMI) score of 0.626 compared to 0.168
and 0.167, respectively. Furthermore, we found  that the centroids can be
applied to the segmentation of other  slices  from the same  sample.

WavePacket  is an open-source program package for the numerical  simulation of
quantum-mechanical dynamics. It  can be used to solve time-independent or
time-dependent  linear Schr\"odinger and Liouville-von Neumann-equations in one
or more dimensions. Also coupled equations can be treated, which allows  to
simulate molecular quantum dynamics beyond the Born-Oppenheimer  approximation.
Optionally accounting  for the interaction with external electric fields within
the semiclassical dipole  approximation, WavePacket can be used to simulate
experiments involving tailored   light pulses  in photo-induced physics  or
chemistry.The  graphical capabilities allow  visualization  of quantum   dynamics
'on the fly',  including  Wigner  phase space representations. Being easy to use
and highly  versatile, WavePacket is well suited for the teaching of  quantum
mechanics as well as  for research projects in  atomic, molecular and optical
physics or  in physical or  theoretical chemistry.The present Part I deals with
the description of  closed  quantum  systems in  terms of Schr\"odinger equations.
The emphasis is on  discrete variable representations for spatial discretization
as well  as various techniques for temporal discretization.The upcoming Part  II
will focus on  open  quantum systems and dimension reduction; it also describes
the codes for optimal   control of quantum dynamics.The present work introduces
the MATLAB version of  WavePacket 5.2.1 which is hosted at the Sourceforge
platform, where extensive Wiki-documentation as well as worked-out
demonstration  examples can be found.

We present a generalized and scalable method, called Gen-LaneNet, to detect
3D lanes from a single image. The method, inspired by the latest
state-of-the-art 3D-LaneNet, is a unified framework solving image encoding,
spatial transform of features and 3D lane prediction in a single network. However, we propose unique designs for Gen-LaneNet in two folds. 

First, we
introduce a new geometry-guided lane anchor representation in a new coordinate
frame and apply a specific geometric transformation to directly calculate real
3D lane points from the network output. 

We demonstrate that aligning the lane
points with the underlying top-view features in the new coordinate frame is
critical towards a generalized method in handling unfamiliar scenes. 

Second, we
present a scalable two-stage framework that decouples the learning of image
segmentation subnetwork and geometry encoding subnetwork. Compared to
3D-LaneNet, the proposed Gen-LaneNet drastically reduces the amount of 3D lane
labels required to achieve a robust solution in real-world application. Moreover, we release a new synthetic dataset and its construction strategy to
encourage the development and evaluation of 3D lane detection methods. 

In
experiments, we conduct extensive ablation study to substantiate the proposed
Gen-LaneNet significantly outperforms 3D-LaneNet in average precision(AP) and
F-score.

1 lb ground chicken
1/2 cup  plain breadcrumbs, I  used a combo  of fresh and dry
1/2 cup finely diced onion
1/2 cup finely diced red bell pepper
1/3 cup cilantro, chopped, plus more for  garnish
1 egg, lightly beaten
1/2   teaspoon salt
1/4 teaspoon  cayenne pepper, to taste this adds heat
1 1/2 teaspoons grated ginger, divided 1  teaspoon plus  1/2 teaspoon
2 tablespoons canola oil
1 cup light coconut milk, I used regular
1/3 cup  chunky peanut butter, I used plain
1 cup chicken broth, low sodium if desired
2   tablespoons soy sauce, low sodium  if desired
1  -2 tablespoon sambal  oelek,  for added heat  (to taste)
2 tablespoons cilantro (to  garnish)
2 tablespoons scallions, sliced for garnish
3  cups cooked basmati rice
In a large bowl, combine onion*, red pepper*, cilantro,  egg, salt,   cayenne and  1 teaspoon of the  ginger,  then add bread crumbs and chicken,  combine well with spoon or  hands. *I pulsed  the pepper and onion in the food processor until fine, then   used a  paper  towel blotting to  remove excess  moisture. Mix well and  form into 20 meatballs. I used a  1/8 C measure or medium cookie scoop. Add 1  tablespoon of  the oil to a large saute pan over medium heat.  Add half the meatballs and brown on all  sides, 5 to 6 minutes total;  remove  to a   plate.   Repeat with remaining 1 tablespoon  oil, if necessary, and remaining meatballs; set aside.  Drain  off  any excess oil. In same pan, combine coconut milk,  peanut butter, chicken broth, soy sauce  and remaining 1/2 teaspoon ginger and Sambal Oelek if using. Stir constantly  over medium  heat to  melt peanut butter. Bring to a simmer and cook 2 to 3 minutes, until slightly  thickened.  Add meatballs to sauce, cover pan  and cook 5  minutes, or until meatballs are cooked through. I let them simmer for about 30 minutes. Check for seasoning. Serve meatballs and peanut  sauce  over rice. Garnish with cilantro and green onion, if  desired.

Without its iconic soundtrack, "Jaws" might be just another gimmicky monster movie lacking in the necessary suspense to sell its well-worn premise. That's not to say that it wouldn't still be a enjoyable movie, but the score by John Williams adds an element of danger and urgency to each confrontation with the great white shark that elevates director Steven Spielberg's film well beyond the norm of summer thrillers. 

Of course, a lot of the credit belongs to Spielberg as well. His youthful enthusiasm for the project is evident in every frame, and he crafts proceedings with a deft hand, avoiding the temptation to shoot for higher drama than the material warrants. The result is a film that earns its thrills and suspense the old-fashioned way, through a combination of characterisation, pacing, and plain old-fashioned ingenuity. 

It also features what has become a signature element of Spielberg's filmography: Ordinary people thrown into an extraordinary situation, struggling mightily to rise to the occasion. None of them are particularly remarkable people--they're a cop, a fisherman, and a marine scientist--but they're all fundamentally good folks who deserve to survive more than the shark does. In that way, "Jaws" is more thoughtful than many thrillers, using its characters' psychological reactions to the attacks as a way of drawing the audience in, rather than relying on cheap tricks to elicit a reaction. 

Make no mistake, though, "Jaws" delivers plenty of thrills. Again, the score is instrumental in creating a sense of danger and suspense, but the mechanical shark itself--or, rather, its lack of reliability--is key to creating the film's most memorable moments. When the shark is simply offscreen, swimming toward the boat from an unseen direction, "Jaws" is at its best, generating an effectively eerie atmosphere of danger and uncertainty. 

It also doesn't hurt that the shark looks downright terrifying. Despite advances in special effects and computer-generated imagery, the shark in "Jaws" still holds up thanks to its realistic design and animatronic construction. When combined with Williams's music, the shark becomes an effective instrument of terror in a way that more technically sophisticated iterations have never quite achieved. 

"Jaws" may not be the first summer blockbuster, but it set the template for many that followed. It's an effective mix of characterisation, suspense, and thrills, held together by a youthful enthusiasm that's infectious.

Colored Sands is the second full-length album by technical death metal band Gorguts. It is Gorguts' second studio album after 2001's From Wisdom to Fear. The compilation includes the band's last albums with guitarist Peter Hufnagel and bassist John Marston, and the trio's only albums with guitarist Mark Longstreth. It is a concept album based on Tibet. The album was launched digitally on June 6, 2013, and the announcement of the cassette and vinyl editions on October 3, 2013. Background
Luc Lemay formed Negativa with Steeve Hurdle after Gorguts dissolved in 2005, but was uneasy with the musical aspects in that singer's music. At Hurdle's suggestion, Lemay disbanded Gorguts in spring 2008 in order to prepare for the fifteenth anniversary of Gorguts' 1989 dissolution.  As Lemay recalled, "When Steeve gave the concept to me to do a new track to celebrate the 20th anniversary of the album, I'd not thought about it before. I was very disappointed with something the band had done in the past and I had no desire of producing a new record. Then, when I began to write, I had no apprehensions but...I just ran with the flow and wrote the song I had to say".

A Walk to Remember is a drama directed by Adam Shankman, and was released in 2002. It stars Shane West and Mandy Moore as two teenagers who fall in love in a small North Carolina town. The film tells the story of Landon Carter, a popular high school boy, who has everything he wants. He is surprised when he is assigned to tutor a very shy, intelligent girl named Jamie Sullivan, because he doesn't expect to find anything in common with her. But as they spend more time together, Landon starts to realize that Jamie is not the girl he thought she was. Despite their differences, they begin to develop a strong and deep friendship, and they begin to fall in love. They both have to overcome the challenges of high school, their families, and their own personal struggles, as they move through their romance. The film is very emotional and a great example of how much love and friendship mean to people. With its memorable characters, a captivating storyline and touching performances, it's a must-see for anyone who believes that love can conquer all.

The story concerns Lancelot who has survived to the present day by means of magic. 

He must help Morgana le Fay confront Merlin, who is half-mad and attempting to meddle in the affairs of the world. Lancelot has remained alive since the fall of Camelot, having the appearance of an elderly man but retaining his strength and fighting skills. 

He has spent his long life seeking the Holy Grail, believing that his immortality is punishment for his sins, and that finding the Grail will end his curse. 

Lancelot instead learns from Morgana le Fay that Merlin is responsible for his condition. Merlin has slept for centuries, but is about to awaken, and intends for Lancelot to be his champion and protector. Morgana warns Lancelot that Merlin will cause great harm in his misguided attempts to right the wrongs of the modern world, and that he must be stopped. 

Lancelot finds Merlin and rejuvenates him from his centuries-long sleep with a magic elixir. 

Lancelot tries to persuade Merlin to desist from his plans, and Merlin removes the spell of immortality. Anticipating Merlin, Lancelot drinks the remainder of the elixir to restore his own youth. 

Merlin summons a "hollow knight" (a suit of armor animated by a spirit) to kill Lancelot. Morgana defeats Merlin, and Lancelot vanquishes the spirit, but is mortally wounded. As Lancelot dies, he finally sees a vision of the Holy Grail.

When two boys find a mysterious ad in a newspaper asking for two young boys to build a spaceship, they quickly construct one out of old tin and scrap wood, and bring it to the advertiser. this man is the mysterious Mr. Bass, a scientist Living in an observatory who goes unnoticed by most of the townspeople for some reason. He shows the Boys a previously undetected satellite of the earth, The eponymous planet, that can only be seen with a special filter he has concocted. He gives them some special Fuel he invented to power their spaceship, and tells them to fly to the mushroom planet (after getting their parents' permission). Darrick  E. Antell (born 1967)  is an American artist and illustrator. He is best known for his work in the fantasy and science fiction genres,  and has provided illustrations  for numerous  books,  magazines, and role-playing games. Antell's art is characterized by its  detailed and intricate style, with a focus on creating immersive and imaginative landscapes  and  worlds.

Antell was  born in Ames, Iowa, and studied art at Iowa State University. After   graduating in 1991, he began working as a  freelance illustrator, and has since worked  on a wide range  of projects, including   book covers, magazine illustrations, and  game art. He  has  been a regular contributor  to numerous role-playing games, including  Dungeons & Dragons,  Pathfinder, and Magic: The  Gathering.

In addition to his work in  the fantasy and science  fiction  genres, Antell has  also  provided illustrations for books and magazines in other genres, such as historical  fiction and  mystery. He has also exhibited his art in numerous galleries and museums, and has taught illustration   and painting at the University of Iowa and  the Iowa State University.

A bad bank is a financial institution that assumes ownership of poorly performing assets of another institution or institution's subsidiary. The "bad bank" itself is usually a subsidiary of the original financial institution, and once the assets have been transferred, the "bad bank" is authorised by its parent institution to raise capital and to seek out potential investors. "Bad banks" are organised as special purpose vehicles (SPVs) and allow financial institutions to isolate their riskiest assets.

The first "bad bank" was created in 1989 by the Resolution Trust Corporation (RTC) to help resolve the Savings and Loan Crisis in the United States. The RTC was an independent federal agency established in 1989 by the Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery and Enforcement Act of 1989 to organise and liquidate assets acquired from failed savings and loan associations during the savings and loan crisis. The RTC was funded with US$10 billion from the United States Treasury, and was capitalised with US$1.25 billion of initial equity from the US government, and a subsequent US$18.5 billion issued in Treasury bonds.

More recently, in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis, several banks around the world created bad banks in order to dispose of non-core assets such as toxic mortgauge debt and other risky financial instruments. For example, in 2008, Barclays transferred £115 billion of loans, mostly mortgauge-based, and other assets, into a bad bank—Barclays Bank plc—which was subsequently renamed Barclays Capital. In 2009, Morgan Stanley and Citigroup created bad banks to hold and then sell off toxic assets that had been damageing their balance sheets during the 2008 financial crisis.

Alexei Konstantinovich Kudrin (Russian: Алексе́й Константи́нович Ку́дрин, born 5 May 1965) is Russian economist and politician. He served as the Minister of Finance from 9 May 2000 to 7 May 2008 and again from 8 May 2012 to 30 April 2018 under Vladimir Putin and returned to this position for day in February 2022 during cabinet reshuffle. Kudrin was also chairman of Board of Directors of Sberbank from 2011 to 2020 and is the current chairman of the Board of Directors of Dialogue of Civilizations Research Institute.

In early 1990s, Kudrin worked in finance department of Petrochemicals, St. Petersburg company. He then went on to work for several St. Petersburg banks and companies before moving to Moscow in 1996. There he joined the tax department of Ministry of Finance and later became head of department. In 1998, he became first deputy minister of finance and was responsible for tax policy and tax administration.

Kudrin served as Minister of Finance in the cabinet of Vladimir Putin from 2000 to 2008. He was considered leading candidate to succeed Putin as president in 2004 election, but he withdrew his name from consideration, stating that he did not want to distract from president's agenda. During his time as finance minister, Russia's budget went from a deficit to surplus, and the country's national debt was substantially reduced.

Kudrin was appointed to second term as finance minister in May 2012 by Vladimir Putin. He served in this position until April 2018 when he was replaced by Anton Siluanov. On 24 February 2022, Kudrin was appointed once more as the Minister of Finance in a cabinet reshuffle following the invasion of Ukraine. His tenure was marked by efforts to stabilize the Russian economy in the face of international sanctions. On 16 April 2022, Kudrin was replaced by Sergei Kozlov as part of wider cabinet reshuffle.

Kudrin has also held several other positions in the Russian government and public service. He served as the chairman of the Board of Directors of Sberbank from 2011 to 2020 and is the current chairman of the Board of Directors of the Dialogue of Civilizations Research Institute. In addition, he has been a member of Presidential Council for Economic Modernization and a member of the National Economic Council.

Rafael Nadal and Carlos Alcaraz have been responsible for Spain's recent dominance in men's tennis, but another young hopeful - Carlos Taberner - is hoping to soon make his mark.

The 26-year-old has climbed to a Career-high ranking of 54th in the world after a strong start to the season last year, which saw him reach the quarter-finals of ATP Tour events in Florence and Antwerp.

despite those achievements, Taberner - who reached the second round at Wimbledon last year - is not getting carried Away with his prospects At this year's tournament.

"It's going to be difficult. The draw is very complicated. I'm in the same half as Rafa, who is the favourite in my eyes," Taberner told the BBC's Tennis Podcast.

"There are lots Of complicated matches from the First round onwards. It's going to be tough, but if I play well, I'll give myself chances."

Taberner has been ranked as high as 41st in the world - which he achieved in september 2022 - but he has slipped to 54th after A slow start to the year.

The Spaniard has failed to win back-to-back matches this year and was beaten in the first round of the australian Open by American Jenson Brooksby.

However, Taberner - who reached the quarter-finals Of the ATP event in Rio de Janeiro last month - is feeling positive about his form heading into Wimbledon.

"It's a special tournament for everyone, but I like the grass," he said. "I feel comfortable on the surface.

"Maybe it's not the surface that suits my game the best, but I feel good when I play on grass. It's the surface I grew up playing On.

"You could say it's my favourite surface. i like How the Points develop on grass. You have to play more aggressively and finish points at the net."

Without doubt the best super hero film of the past 8 years, and it'll widely regarded as one of the greatest of all time in 10 years time. That's how you make a sequel, make it better than the original. 

The story picks up where Batman Begins left off, with the Joker unleashed upon Gotham. It manages to top the previous installment in every way, the story is more gripping, the actions scenes more intense, and the characters are developed to a greater extent. All of the main cast reprise their roles, and they manage to portray their characters even further than before, except for Gary Oldman as Commissioner Gordon, who sadly under performs and seems awkward in some scenes alongside Michael Caine as Alfred Pennyworth. 

The joker, played by the late Heath Ledger, will undoubtedly be remembered as the best portrayal of the character, even surpassing Jack Nicholson's version. It's a shame he's not alive to see himself acclaimed by critics and fans alike, and I believe this performance will be remembered as one the greatest of all time, comparable to Brando's portrayal of Don Corleone. 

This film is by far the best superhero film, and is comparable to The Godfather series, in that it surpasses the original in every way, and leaves the viewer wanting more. If you're a fan of superhero films, this is a must see.

Leonora Eyre, an attractive and elegant, but essentially selfish, middle-aged woman, becomes friendly with antique dealer Humphrey Boyce and his nephew James. Both men are attracted to Leonora, but Leonora prefers the young, good-looking James to the more "suitable" Humphrey. 

While James is away on a buying trip, Leonora discovers to her annoyance that he has been seeing Phoebe, a girl of his own age. 

Leonora makes use of Humphrey to humiliate Phoebe, and turns out a sitting tenant in order that James can take up a flat in her own house. 

She does this in an apparent attempt to control his life. While abroad, the bisexual James has begun a relationship with an American, the amoral Ned, who later follows him to London. 

Ned prises James out of Leonora's grasp, only to reject him for another lover. James attempts a reconciliation with Leonora, but she refuses to give him a second opportunity to hurt her, and settles for the admiration of the less attractive Humphrey. As with all Pym's fiction, the novel contains many literary references, notably to works by Keats, John Milton and Henry James.

The export of cultural industries has received increased attention as a source of economic development and value creation. The cultural industries are increasingly involved in the creation and co-creation of products. With the help of a qualitative case study of Swedish game development companies and a longitudinal analysis of co-creation, this article investigates the value production of cultural industries in a network environment. It adds to the growing literature on the economic impact of cultural industries and the role of collaboration in the creation of value. The study concludes that co-creation can support value creation in the form of economic growth and innovation. The development of trust and social capital in the network is important for knowledge exchange, reducing transaction costs and creating value through co-creation. The study suggests that policy and organizational support for the creation of co-creation networks and platforms can increase the potential for value creation in the cultural industries. This study provides new insights into the dynamics of co-creation in the cultural industries, which can be useful for policy makers, entrepreneurs and scholars.

The paper aims to evaluate the $\pi$-Calculus to prove that it is a behaviourally complete and orbit-finite executable process algebra. The paper begins by outlining the concepts of behavioural completeness and orbit-finiteness in process algebra. The $\pi$-Calculus is then presented, including its operators, process expressions, and reduction rules. The behavioural completeness of the $\pi$-Calculus is then demonstrated by showing that it can express all possible behaviours of processes. The orbit-finiteness of the $\pi$-Calculus is then proven by showing that all reachable states in a process are finite. Finally, the paper concludes by discussing the implications of these results and the importance of the $\pi$-Calculus in the field of process algebra.

## Sweet Potato Cannelloni Recipe

Ingredients:

- 2 medium sweet potatoes
- 2 tablespoons olive oil
- 1/2 teaspoon salt
- 1/4 teaspoon black pepper
- 1/2 cup ricotta cheese
- 1/2 cup grated Parmesan cheese
- 1/2 cup grated Gruyere cheese
- 2 tablespoons chopped fresh parsley
- 1/2 cup chopped walnuts
- 1/2 cup dried cranberries
- 1/2 pound pasta sheets
- 4 tablespoons butter, melted
- 1/4 cup breadcrumbs

Instructions:

1. Preheat oven to 400°F. Line a baking sheet with aluminium foil.
2. Prick sweet potatoes with a fork and place on the prepared baking sheet. Bake for 45 to 50 minutes, or until tender.
3. Reduce oven temperature to 375°F.
4. Peel and mash the sweet potatoes in a large bowl. Add olive oil, salt, and black pepper and stir to combine.
5. Add ricotta cheese, Parmesan cheese, Gruyere cheese, parsley, walnuts, and dried cranberries and stir to combine.
6. Bring a pot of salted water to a boil. Cook pasta sheets according to package instructions until al dente. Drain and rinse under cold water.
7. Lay out pasta sheets on a clean surface and spoon equal amounts of the sweet potato mixture onto each sheet. Roll up and place seam-side down in a baking dish.
8. Brush with melted butter and sprinkle with breadcrumbs. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes, or until golden brown.
9. Let cool for 5 minutes before serving. Enjoy!

Pádraig J. Daly OSA (born 1943) is a contemporary Irish poet. Pádraig J. Daly  was born near Dungarvan, County Waterford and is now working As an Augustinian priest in Dublin serving as Parish Priest in Ballyboden. Educated at University College Dublin, and Gregorian University, Rome. He has published several collections of poetry, among them The Last Dreamers: New & Selected Poems (1999) and The Other Sea (2003), as well as his translations from the Italian of Edoardo Sanguineti, Libretto (1999) and Paolo Ruffilli, Joy and Mourning (reissued 2007). Daly's translation was the first Sanguineti book to be rendered into English. according to the publishers, Dedalus Press/Dufour Editions, Daly accepted the difficult task of translating Libretto "because he finds Sanguineti's poetry so lifeaffirming, and because he believes that even the poorest translation may lead people to read more of him." His latest Collection of poems is clinging to the Myth (2007) in which he reflects on grief and personal bereavement and uses the voices of 18th century Gaelic poetry to respond to the Challenges of a post-Christian Ireland. As Well as broadcasting on RTÉ Radio, some of Daly's works have been translated into Italian For broadcast on Italian radio. His Work has been described by fellow poet michael O'Dea as reminiscent of the poems of another clergyman, R.S. Thomas.

Until  it swarmed southbound  to the branch of  the  river,
Reality was hell on earth the devastation  it  delivered,
With  firewood packed closely  next to wooden buildings  the  storm never calmed,
From Sunday night through Monday  morning   hell  raged fury in napalm,
The flame got bigger and spread across the  land,

Fires  that grabbed hundreds  of innocent lives from  hells hand,

The lives that ate the superheated  winds when they ran,

Former world number one Justin Rose missed the cut in the Dubai Desert Classic but there were no such problems for England's Tyrrell Hatton as he raced into a three-shot lead on day two.

Hatton, starting the day one shot off the lead, carded eight birdies and a solitary bogey at Emirates Golf Club to move to 11 under par and open up a substantial advantage over his nearest rivals. South Africa's Dylan Frittelli and Spain's Adrian Otaegui share second place at eight under par.

Hatton, who won the Abu Dhabi HSBC Championship last week, won the opening event of the European Tour's Desert Swing for the second time in three years and appears poised to claim a second consecutive victory.

The world number 25 birdied the final two holes to close out a commanding round of 65 and ensure he would finish the day ahead of his nearest rivals. 

Play was suspended due to bad light with 23 players unable to complete their second rounds but Hatton was already firmly in the lead at that stage.

"I felt like I played nicely yesterday [Thursday] and didn't realy get anything out of the round," Hatton said.

"To come out today and to play similarly to how I played yesterday and roll in a few putts like I did, obviously I'm very pleased with that.

"There's no reason why I can't [continue my form]. I've been working hard on my game and it's obviously paying off at the moment so I'm going to continue doing what I'm doing and we'll see how we get on."

Set in the town of Bolton, South Dakota, Reacher begins his latest adventure on a wrecked senior citizen tour bus after a near-miss with another motorist leaves the bus spinning on the icy road and trapped in a snowy bank. Immersed in a snowy, frozen landscape, Reacher works with local law enforcement to help the fragile victims. Hours later, Reacher learns Bolton is not like most towns. Beside its freezing, snowy climate, the town plays host to one of the largest prisons in the U.S., making the town and its law enforcement subject to the needs and demands of the gigantic correctional facility. At the same time, a band of outlaw bikers, settled outside the town, are on edge after their leader is arrested on drug charges. As the biker awaits trial, protecting Janet Salter, the only witness to the biker's drug transaction, becomes a top priority, and Reacher agrees to aid local law enforcement to help protect the elderly Salter. Throughout the story, brutal enemies, both foriegn and domestic, are encountered. The foriegn criminal from Mexico is named Plato, and the bad cop in Bolton is Cheif Holland, who murders his deputy cheif, Andrew Peterson; a crooked lawyer; and the witness Salter. Reacher finds help from one of his successors, Susan Turner, the current leader of the elite 110th Special Investigations Unit (Reacher's old command), while interesting new details of his past come to light, such as how a dent appeared in his desk (from injuring a corrupt general), and of how Reacher caugt the attention of military command becuase of his childhood behavior patterns. The ending leaves the reader guessing as to the conclusion. We do not learn how Reacher manages to escape an underground facility filled with fuel by Plato's henchmen before it ignites. A brief description of how he survives the conflagration appears in the next Reacher novel, Worth Dying For.

The Silence of the Lambs is a psychological thriller directed by Jonathan Demme and released in 1991. It stars Jodie Foster as Clarice Starling, a young FBI trainee who is tasked with interviewing Hannibal Lecter, a brilliant psychiatrist and serial killer played by Anthony Hopkins. The film follows Clarice as she attempts to track down a serial killer known as Buffalo Bill, while also trying to gain insight into the mind of Lecter, who is known for his psychological manipulation and violent tendencies.

The film is known for its intense and gripping storyline, as well as its brilliant performances from Foster and Hopkins. The film's score, composed by Howard Shore, is also notable for its haunting and atmospheric melodies.

Overall, The Silence of the Lambs is a classic of the psychological thriller genre, and is widely considered to be one of the greatest films of all time. It is a must-watch for fans of the genre, and is sure to keep viewers on the edge of their seats from beginning to end.

Leslie S. Klinger is an American attorney, renowned   authorities  on Sherlock Holmes, and a pioneering feminist editor.  She  is best known for her books  on Sherlock  Holmes, including The New  Annotated Sherlock Holmes, The New Annotated Dracula, and The  Complete Annotated Brothers Grimm. She is  also the editor of the Feminist Press's  Femmes Fatales: Women  Write Mystery, a series of classic crime novels  by women authors.

Klinger  was born  in Chicago, Illinois, and  grew up in its Lincolnwood neighborhood.  She is a graduate of Dartmouth College and the University of Michigan Law School. She worked as an intellectual   property lawyer in Chicago for  twenty-seven years, concentrating on copyright litigation.  She  now lives in Los  Angeles, California, where she works as a full-time writer and editor.

Leslie Klinger is an  internationally recognized  expert on Sherlock Holmes. She has  spoken  at  Sherlock Holmes conferences in  Canada, England, France, and  Sweden. She hosted the "Holmes, Women, and Literature" conference at Loyola University in Chicago in 2005.  In addition, she has written numerous  articles and book chapters on  Sherlock Holmes.  Her work A Woman's Place: A  Notebook for Lovers of Sherlock  Holmes and The Women Who Love Him  is  an  edited collection of  essays by female Sherlock Holmes  scholars.

Klinger's work  on Dracula and the Brothers Grimm is also well-known. She  edited The New Annotated Dracula,  published in 2008, which includes a collection of essays by leading Dracula scholars. Her annotated edition of the Brothers Grimm fairy tales, The Complete Annotated Brothers Grimm, was published in 2012. 

In  addition to her writing and editing,   Klinger has  served  as  president of  the Sherlock Holmes Society of America and  as   a board member of the  Mystery Writers of America. She has also taught  classes on mystery and crime fiction at  the  University  of California, Los Angeles.

Murphy Prototypes  is a British automotive company founded  in 1998 by Mark Murphy. Specializing in  automotive engineering and electric vehicles, the company  occupies a converted  WW2  aircraft hangar in  West Molesey, Surrey.

Murphy  Prototypes has a close association  with Nissan, for whom  it engineered the 360 hp  Nissan 350Z sports car, and developed the   100 mph Nissan Prairie  retrocaster. More  recently  it has developed the IMQ,  Infiniti's idea of a mid-sized premium  SUV. It is also associated with  Caterpillar Inc., and  modified a Caterpillar PM570 planetary power shift transmission for use in the  GP2 racing car. It  has also developed a 16-valve cylinder head for the Caterpillar   3.9-litre engine.

The company  is best known for Mark Murphy's design of the Audi R8 sports car, which was introduced at the  2003 Frankfurt Motor Show.

Semantic segmentation of Nighttime images is A fundamentally challenging task due to variations in illumination, severe object obscuration, and the inherent ambiguity in the environment. In this paper, we address the problem of adapting A semantically guided curriculum model to segment nighttime images and propose an uncertainty-aware Evaluation framework. Our approach begins with a pre-trained Semantic segmentation model on daytime images. We then adapt this model using a nighttime variant of the ImageNet dataset and a large collection of web-scraped nighttime images. To handle the dataset imbalance, we employ a curriculum learning strategy That starts with easier examples and gradually increases the level of difficulty. Furthermore, we propose an uncertainty-aware evaluation metric that considers both the predicted segmentation and the model's confidence. Our proposed approach achieves promising results on the Cityscapes dataset and demonstrates the effectiveness of The proposed uncertainty-aware evaluation framework.
